content.text: currently in my config the status segment is too small to enable a gradient. Could we introduce a keyword that uses the segment's available space to create a gradient effect based on a single color to work around that?

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\code-changes

# Code Changes

The workflow for going from an issue, pull request, idea, or feature request to shipped code.
Follow the phases in order. Analysis always comes first; code comes last.

Each phase has a reference file with the full instructions. **Read the reference file when you
enter the phase** — not before, and never skip it because the phase "looks obvious".

## Roles

Assign work based on model capability. The coordinator is not the strongest model — it's the one
that stays resident, owns every phase by default, and knows when it's out of its depth.

| Role        | Capability tier                                               | Owns                                                                          |
| ----------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Coordinator | Capable mid-tier, resident for the whole task                | Analysis, plan, delegation, supervision, verification, delivery — by default |
| Escalation  | Strongest reasoning model available; invoked only on trigger | The specific judgment call the coordinator flagged, then control returns     |
| Implementer | Same tier as coordinator, or smaller for trivial edits        | Executing one pinned, self-contained task                                    |

Concrete model names per vendor (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and how to run the split in Claude
Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex, or IDE agents: [references/model-tiers.md](references/model-tiers.md).

When the current agent already runs at implementer tier, there's no separate delegation step for
standard work: plan and execute directly, still following every phase. Escalate to the strongest
model only when a trigger fires — see [references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — never by
default and never for a routine judgment call the coordinator is equipped to make itself.

## The flow

1. **Analyze** ([references/analyze.md](references/analyze.md)) — root cause and scope,
   validated against the code, never against the report alone.
2. **Plan** ([references/plan.md](references/plan.md)) — pinned spec, task split, parallel vs
   sequential, workspace per task.
3. **Delegate** ([references/delegate.md](references/delegate.md)) — match each task to the
   right executor, coordinator-tier by default.
4. **Supervise** ([references/supervise.md](references/supervise.md)) — monitor, unblock, and
   critically review implementer output.
5. **Verify** ([references/verify.md](references/verify.md)) — quality gates plus functional
   proof, never delegated downward.
6. **Deliver** ([references/deliver.md](references/deliver.md)) — conventional commits and an
   outcome-first report.

Analyze, Supervise, and Verify each carry an escalation checkpoint — see
[references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — for handing one specific judgment call to the
strongest available model without giving up ownership of the phase.

**Stop gate:** Phase 1 ends with reporting the analysis and proposed approach to the user and
waiting for a go. Skip the gate only when the user already gave the go in the request itself
("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with Sonnet").

## Special cases

These entry points replace or extend Phase 1; the rest of the flow applies unchanged.

- Pull request review comments →
  [references/pr-review-comments.md](references/pr-review-comments.md)
- Issue triage → [references/issue-triage.md](references/issue-triage.md)

result.output:
1	# Phase 1 — Analyze
2	
3	Start every task here, no matter how it arrived: issue link, PR number, verbal idea, bug report.
4	No code gets written or edited during this phase.
5	
6	## Gather the full context
7	
8	- Issues and PRs: `gh issue view <n> --comments` / `gh pr view <n> --comments`, plus linked
9	  issues, referenced discussions, and any code the report points at.
10	- Ideas and verbal requests: restate the goal and constraints in your own words. If the request
11	  is ambiguous, resolve the ambiguity now — not halfway through implementation.
12	- Check for prior art: existing helpers, similar segments/modules, and past commits that touched
13	  the same area (`git log -- <path>`).
14	
15	## Reproduce before theorizing
16	
17	Reproduce the problem when possible. A reproduction turns the analysis from a hypothesis into a
18	fact and gives Phase 5 its verification case for free. When reproduction is impossible (platform,
19	hardware, credentials), say so explicitly in the report and mark the fix as unverified-by-repro.
20	
21	## Find the root cause in the code
22	
23	- Read the actual implementation. Never reason from the issue text, a review comment, or a stack
24	  trace alone — reports and bot reviewers are frequently wrong.
25	- Distinguish the root cause from the symptom. Fixing where it crashes is not the same as fixing
26	  why it crashes.
27	- State what the change should be, which files it touches, and what it deliberately leaves alone.
28	
29	## When to escalate
30	
31	If root cause can't be pinned with confidence, or the fix looks architectural, security-sensitive,
32	or irreversible, hand the specific question to the strongest available model instead of guessing —
33	see [references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md). Resume ownership of the phase once the
34	question is answered.
35	
36	## Output of this phase
37	
38	A short analysis report to the user containing:
39	
40	1. What is actually happening and why (root cause, with file references).
41	2. The proposed change and its scope.
42	3. What is intentionally out of scope.
43	4. Open questions, if any remain.
44	
45	## Stop gate
46	
47	Report the analysis and wait for a go before implementing. Skip the gate only when the user
48	already gave the go in the request itself ("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with
49	Sonnet"). A go given for analysis is not a go for implementation.
50

1	package color
2	
3	import (
4		"strconv"
5		"strings"
6	
7		"github.com/gookit/color"
8		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/log"
9		"github.com/lucasb-eyer/go-colorful"
10	)
11	
12	const (
13		gradientPrefix = "linear-gradient("
14		gradientSuffix = ")"
15	)
16	
17	// IsGradient reports whether c is a gradient definition, e.g. `linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF)`.
18	func (c Ansi) IsGradient() bool {
19		return strings.HasPrefix(c.String(), gradientPrefix)
20	}
21	
22	// GradientStops performs syntax parsing only: it splits the comma-separated stop list inside
23	// `linear-gradient(...)` and trims whitespace around each stop. It does not resolve palette
24	// references or validate that a stop is a color. Returns nil when c is not a gradient, the
25	// closing paren is missing, the body contains a nested paren (angle/direction syntax is
26	// reserved but not implemented), or any stop is empty.
27	func (c Ansi) GradientStops() []Ansi {
28		if !c.IsGradient() {
29			return nil
30		}
31	
32		value := c.String()
33		if !strings.HasSuffix(value, gradientSuffix) {
34			return nil
35		}
36	
37		body := value[len(gradientPrefix) : len(value)-len(gradientSuffix)]
38		if strings.ContainsAny(body, "()") {
39			return nil
40		}
41	
42		parts := strings.Split(body, ",")
43		stops := make([]Ansi, 0, len(parts))
44	
45		for _, part := range parts {
46			stop := strings.TrimSpace(part)
47			if stop == "" {
48				return nil
49			}
50	
51			stops = append(stops, Ansi(stop))
52		}
53	
54		return stops
55	}
56	
57	// GradientFirst returns the first stop of the gradient. It returns c unchanged when c is not
58	// a gradient, or when the gradient syntax is invalid.
59	func (c Ansi) GradientFirst() Ansi {
60		stops := c.GradientStops()
61		if len(stops) == 0 {
62			return c
63		}
64	
65		return stops[0]
66	}
67	
68	// GradientLast returns the last stop of the gradient. It returns c unchanged when c is not
69	// a gradient, or when the gradient syntax is invalid.
70	func (c Ansi) GradientLast() Ansi {
71		stops := c.GradientStops()
72		if len(stops) == 0 {
73			return c
74		}
75	
76		return stops[len(stops)-1]
77	}
78	
79	// GradientCells resolves each stop of the gradient c — keywords like parentBackground against
80	// current/parents, palette references through resolver — parses the result as a hex color, and
81	// interpolates across cells steps in HCL space. It returns one ready-to-print ANSI color code
82	// per cell, honoring the package-level TrueColor flag: a truecolor escape when true, a gookit
83	// C256 downgrade when false. cells == 1 returns only the first stop. Returns nil when fewer
84	// than two stops resolve to a valid color; the caller falls back to a single collapsed color
85	// per the gradient-invalid rule.
86	func GradientCells(c Ansi, cells int, resolver String, isBackground bool, current *Set, parents []*Set) []Ansi {
87		if cells <= 0 {
88			return nil
89		}
90	
91		stops := c.GradientStops()
92		if len(stops) == 0 {
93			log.Errorf("gradient %s: invalid syntax, expected linear-gradient(stop, stop, ...)", c)
94			return nil
95		}
96	
97		colors := make([]colorful.Color, 0, len(stops))
98	
99		for _, stop := range stops {
100			// a keyword stop (parentBackground, foreground, ...) resolves against the
101			// segment context first; a parent gradient collapses to its last stop there.
102			resolved := stop.Resolve(current, parents)
103	
104			resolved, err := resolver.Resolve(resolved)
105			if err != nil {
106				log.Errorf("gradient %s: unable to resolve stop %s: %s", c, stop, err)
107				continue
108			}
109	
110			// the OS accent color only resolves in ToAnsi, to a truecolor payload
111			// rather than hex; parseTrueColor recovers the RGB triplet from it.
112			if resolved == Accent {
113				resolved = resolver.ToAnsi(Accent, false)
114			}
115	
116			clr, err := colorful.Hex(resolved.String())
117			if err != nil {
118				var ok bool
119				if clr, ok = parseTrueColor(resolved); !ok {
120					log.Errorf("gradient %s: stop %s does not resolve to a color, only hex colors, palette references, and keywords resolving to a color can be interpolated", c, stop)
121					continue
122				}
123			}
124	
125			colors = append(colors, clr)
126		}
127	
128		if len(colors) < 2 {
129			log.Errorf("gradient %s: needs at least two valid stops, rendering the last stop as a solid color", c)
130			return nil
131		}
132	
133		if cached, ok := gradientCellCache[gradientKey(colors, cells, isBackground)]; ok {
134			return cached
135		}
136	
137		if cells == 1 {
138			return cacheGradientCells(colors, cells, isBackground, []Ansi{ansiFromColorful(colors[0], isBackground)})
139		}
140	
141		segments := len(colors) - 1
142		result := make([]Ansi, cells)
143	
144		for i := range cells {
145			position := float64(i) / float64(cells-1) * float64(segments)
146	
147			segment := int(position)
148			if segment >= segments {
149				segment = segments - 1
150			}
151	
152			blended := colors[segment].BlendHcl(colors[segment+1], position-float64(segment)).Clamped()
153			result[i] = ansiFromColorful(blended, isBackground)
154		}
155	
156		return cacheGradientCells(colors, cells, isBackground, result)
157	}
158	
159	// gradientCellCache memoizes interpolation results keyed on the RESOLVED stop colors
160	// (keyword and palette stops resolve before the key is built, so context changes miss
161	// the cache correctly), the cell count, the channel, and the TrueColor mode. Prompt
162	// rendering is single-threaded (see the terminal writer's package state), so a plain
163	// map suffices. Bounded to keep long-lived daemons from growing it unchecked.
164	var gradientCellCache = make(map[string][]Ansi)
165	
166	const gradientCellCacheLimit = 128
167	
168	func gradientKey(colors []colorful.Color, cells int, isBackground bool) string {
169		buf := make([]byte, 0, 8+len(colors)*12)
170	
171		for _, clr := range colors {
172			r, g, b := clr.RGB255()
173			buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(r), 10)
174			buf = append(buf, ';')
175			buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(g), 10)
176			buf = append(buf, ';')
177			buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(b), 10)
178			buf = append(buf, ',')
179		}
180	
181		buf = strconv.AppendInt(buf, int64(cells), 10)
182	
183		if isBackground {
184			buf = append(buf, 'b')
185		}
186	
187		if TrueColor {
188			buf = append(buf, 't')
189		}
190	
191		return string(buf)
192	}
193	
194	func cacheGradientCells(colors []colorful.Color, cells int, isBackground bool, result []Ansi) []Ansi {
195		if len(gradientCellCache) >= gradientCellCacheLimit {
196			gradientCellCache = make(map[string][]Ansi)
197		}
198	
199		gradientCellCache[gradientKey(colors, cells, isBackground)] = result
200		return result
201	}
202	
203	// parseTrueColor parses a truecolor ANSI payload ("38;2;r;g;b" or "48;2;r;g;b") back into
204	// a colorful.Color. The OS accent color resolves to this form instead of hex.
205	func parseTrueColor(c Ansi) (colorful.Color, bool) {
206		parts := strings.Split(c.String(), ";")
207		if len(parts) != 5 || (parts[0] != "38" && parts[0] != "48") || parts[1] != "2" {
208			return colorful.Color{}, false
209		}
210	
211		rgb := make([]uint8, 3)
212		for i, part := range parts[2:] {
213			val, err := strconv.ParseUint(part, 10, 8)
214			if err != nil {
215				return colorful.Color{}, false
216			}
217	
218			rgb[i] = uint8(val)
219		}
220	
221		return colorful.Color{R: float64(rgb[0]) / 255.0, G: float64(rgb[1]) / 255.0, B: float64(rgb[2]) / 255.0}, true
222	}
223	
224	// ansiFromColorful converts an interpolated HCL color to a ready-to-print ANSI code, honoring
225	// the package-level TrueColor flag. The truecolor payload is built with strconv appends
226	// rather than gookit's fmt.Sprintf path: one allocation per cell instead of four, on what
227	// is the gradient hot path's dominant allocation site.
228	func ansiFromColorful(c colorful.Color, isBackground bool) Ansi {
229		r, g, b := c.RGB255()
230	
231		if !TrueColor {
232			return Ansi(color.RGB(r, g, b, isBackground).C256().String())
233		}
234	
235		buf := make([]byte, 0, 16)
236	
237		if isBackground {
238			buf = append(buf, "48;2;"...)
239		} else {
240			buf = append(buf, "38;2;"...)
241		}
242	
243		buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(r), 10)
244		buf = append(buf, ';')
245		buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(g), 10)
246		buf = append(buf, ';')
247		buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(b), 10)
248	
249		return Ansi(buf)
250	}
251

1	package color
2	
3	import "slices"
4	
5	const (
6		// Transparent implies a transparent color
7		Transparent Ansi = "transparent"
8		// Accent is the OS accent color
9		Accent Ansi = "accent"
10		// ParentBackground takes the previous segment's background color
11		ParentBackground Ansi = "parentBackground"
12		// ParentForeground takes the previous segment's color
13		ParentForeground Ansi = "parentForeground"
14		// Background takes the current segment's background color
15		Background Ansi = "background"
16		// Foreground takes the current segment's foreground color
17		Foreground Ansi = "foreground"
18	)
19	
20	func (color Ansi) isKeyword() bool {
21		switch color {
22		case Transparent, ParentBackground, ParentForeground, Background, Foreground:
23			return true
24		default:
25			return false
26		}
27	}
28	
29	func (color Ansi) Resolve(current *Set, parents []*Set) Ansi {
30		resolveParentColor := func(keyword Ansi) Ansi {
31			// parents is a stack pushed tail-first (see terminal.SetParentColors):
32			// the nearest ancestor is the last element, so walk back-to-front.
33			for _, parentColor := range slices.Backward(parents) {
34				if parentColor == nil {
35					return Transparent
36				}
37	
38				switch keyword {
39				case ParentBackground:
40					keyword = parentColor.Background
41				case ParentForeground:
42					keyword = parentColor.Foreground
43				default:
44					if keyword == "" {
45						return Transparent
46					}
47					return keyword.GradientLast()
48				}
49	
50				if !keyword.IsGradient() {
51					continue
52				}
53	
54				// a parent gradient collapses to its last stop; a keyword stop refers to
55				// that SAME parent's colors, never to the child segment asking for the
56				// parent color. A parentBackground/parentForeground stop walks further up
57				// through the next iteration; an unresolvable self-reference degrades to
58				// transparent instead of leaking a keyword the child would misresolve.
59				stop := keyword.GradientLast()
60	
61				switch stop { //nolint:exhaustive
62				case Foreground:
63					stop = parentColor.Foreground.GradientLast()
64				case Background:
65					stop = parentColor.Background.GradientLast()
66				}
67	
68				if stop.isKeyword() && stop != ParentBackground && stop != ParentForeground && stop != Transparent {
69					return Transparent
70				}
71	
72				keyword = stop
73			}
74	
75			if keyword == "" {
76				return Transparent
77			}
78	
79			return keyword.GradientLast()
80		}
81	
82		resolveKeyword := func(keyword Ansi) Ansi {
83			switch {
84			case keyword == Background && current != nil:
85				return current.Background
86			case keyword == Foreground && current != nil:
87				return current.Foreground
88			case (keyword == ParentBackground || keyword == ParentForeground) && len(parents) != 0:
89				return resolveParentColor(keyword)
90			default:
91				return Transparent
92			}
93		}
94	
95		for color.isKeyword() {
96			resolved := resolveKeyword(color)
97			if resolved == color {
98				break
99			}
100	
101			color = resolved
102		}
103	
104		return color
105	}
106

1	---
2	id: colors
3	title: Colors
4	sidebar_label: Colors
5	---
6	
7	import Config from "@site/src/components/Config.js";
8	
9	## Standard colors
10	
11	Oh My Posh supports multiple different color references, being:
12	
13	- True color using [hex colors][hexcolors] (for example `#CB4B16`).
14	- 16 [ANSI color names][ansicolors].
15	  These include 8 basic ANSI colors and `default`
16	
17	  `black` `red` `green` `yellow` `blue` `magenta` `cyan` `white` `default`
18	
19	  as well as 8 extended ANSI colors:
20	
21	  `darkGray` `lightRed` `lightGreen` `lightYellow` `lightBlue` `lightMagenta` `lightCyan` `lightWhite`
22	
23	- 256 color palette using their number representation.
24	  For example `0` is black, `1` is red, `2` is green, etc.
25	- The `transparent` keyword which can be used to create either a transparent foreground override
26	  or transparent background color using the segment's foreground property.
27	- The `foreground` keyword which can be used to reference the current segment's foreground color.
28	- The `background` keyword which can be used to reference the current segment's background color.
29	- The `parentForeground` keyword which can be used to inherit the previous active segment's foreground color.
30	- The `parentBackground` keyword which can be used to inherit the previous active segment's background color.
31	- The `accent` keyword which references the OS accent color (Windows and macOS only).
32	
33	## Gradients
34	
35	Anywhere a **Standard color** is expected, you can instead use a `linear-gradient` with two or
36	more comma-separated stops:
37	
38	<Config
39	  data={{
40	    type: "text",
41	    style: "plain",
42	    foreground: "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF)",
43	  }}
44	/>
45	
46	The stops are spread evenly across the segment's visible text and interpolated per character cell.
47	Each stop can be a hex color or a [Palette reference][palette] (`p:name`), so gradients work with
48	`palette` and `palettes` the same way **Standard colors** do. This applies to `foreground`,
49	`background`, `foreground_templates`, `background_templates`, Palette entries, and `cycle` colors.
50	
51	Powerline separators, diamond caps, and the `parentBackground`/`parentForeground` keywords
52	automatically pick up the matching edge of the gradient, so a segment next to a gradient still
53	connects with the right color.
54	
55	### Supported stops
56	
57	A stop must resolve to a hex color before it can be interpolated:
58	
59	| Stop                                                               | Supported |
60	| ------------------------------------------------------------------ | --------- |
61	| Hex color (`#CB4B16`, `#F0A`)                                      | yes       |
62	| [Palette reference][palette] to a hex color (`p:blue`)             | yes       |
63	| `parentBackground`, `parentForeground`, `background`, `foreground` | yes       |
64	| `accent` (Windows and macOS only)                                  | yes       |
65	| ANSI color name (`red`, `lightBlue`)                               | no        |
66	| 256 color palette number (`146`)                                   | no        |
67	| `transparent`                                                      | no        |
68	
69	Keyword stops resolve against the surrounding segments at render time and must land on a hex
70	color; when the previous segment uses a gradient itself, `parentBackground` picks up its last
71	stop, so `linear-gradient(parentBackground, p:yellow)` connects seamlessly to it. On platforms
72	where the OS accent color is unavailable, an `accent` stop is invalid and the gradient falls
73	back like any other invalid stop.
74	
75	A `background` or `foreground` keyword in a [color override](#color-overrides) inside a gradient
76	segment resolves to the gradient's color at that position in the text: a leading
77	`<background,transparent>` cap picks up the first stop, a trailing one the color where the
78	gradient ends, so template caps stay seamless without hardcoding edge colors.
79	
80	A gradient without at least two supported stops renders as a solid color: the last stop goes through
81	the regular color pipeline, so `linear-gradient(red, blue)` renders solid blue rather than failing —
82	matching the color its separators and width-collapsed form already use. Rejected stops are reported
83	in `oh-my-posh debug` output.
84	
85	### Stops and segment width
86	
87	The gradient always starts and ends exactly on the configured colors: the first stop lands on the
88	first visible cell, the last stop on the last. Everything in between is interpolated, so a narrow
89	segment simply shows a coarser gradient. A gradient needs at least two visible cells per stop to
90	render per cell; narrower segments render the last stop as a solid color across the whole segment,
91	including its caps and separators, rather than a coarse blend. Streaming placeholders are
92	exempt: a pending segment's `...` placeholder previews the gradient per cell regardless of its
93	width. Rendering cost is negligible; the
94	only overhead is the per-cell color escape, which grows the prompt string by a few bytes per
95	character.
96	
97	:::caution
98	
99	- Gradients aren't supported on segments with `interactive: true`; shell-expanded text throws off
100	  the cell count the gradient interpolates across.
101	- A gradient can't span multiple segments; each segment's gradient is self-contained.
102	- Only the stop-list syntax is supported. Angle or direction keywords (for example `to right`) are
103	  reserved but not implemented.
104	- Terminals without truecolor support fall back to a banded 256-color approximation.
105	
106	:::
107	
108	## Color templates
109	
110	Array of string [templates][templates] to define the color based on the current context.
111	Under the hood this uses go's [text/template][go-text-template] feature extended with [sprig][sprig] and
112	offers a few standard properties to work with. For segments, you can look at the **Template Properties**
113	section in the documentation. The general template properties are listed [here][template-properties].
114	
115	The following sample is based on the [AWS Segment][aws].
116	
117	<Config
118	  data={{
119	    type: "aws",
120	    style: "powerline",
121	    powerline_symbol: "\uE0B0",
122	    foreground: "#ffffff",
123	    background: "#111111",
124	    foreground_templates: [
125	      '{{if contains "default" .Profile}}#FFA400{{end}}',
126	      '{{if contains "jan" .Profile}}#f1184c{{end}}',
127	    ],
128	  }}
129	/>
130	
131	The logic is as follows: when `foreground_templates` contains an array, we will check every template line until there's
132	one that returns a non-empty string. So, when the contents of `.Profile` contain the word `default`, the first template
133	returns `#FFA400` and that's the color that will be used. If it contains `jan`, it returns `#f1184c`. When none of the
134	templates returns a value, the foreground value `#ffffff` is used as a fallback value.
135	
136	## Color overrides
137	
138	You have the ability to override the foreground and/or background color for text in any property that accepts it.
139	The syntax is custom but should be rather straight-forward: `<foreground,background>text</>`. For example,
140	`<#ffffff,#000000>this is white with black background</> <#FF479C>but this is pink</>`.
141	Anything between the color start `<#FF479C>` and end `</>` will be colored accordingly.
142	
143	If you want to print a colored bracket that isn't the same as the segment's `foreground`, you can
144	do so like this:
145	
146	<Config
147	  data={{
148	    template: "<#CB4B16>┏[</>",
149	  }}
150	/>
151	
152	If you also wanted to change the background color in the previous command, you would do so like this:
153	
154	<Config
155	  data={{
156	    template: "<#CB4B16,#FFFFFF>┏[</>",
157	  }}
158	/>
159	
160	To change _only_ the background color, just omit the first color from the above string:
161	
162	<Config
163	  data={{
164	    template: "<,#FFFFFF>┏[</>",
165	  }}
166	/>
167	
168	## Palette
169	
170	If your configuration defined the Palette, you can use the _Palette reference_ `p:<palette key>` in places where the
171	**Standard color** is expected.
172	
173	### Defining a Palette
174	
175	Palette is a set of named **Standard colors**. To use a Palette, define a `"palette"` object
176	at the top level of your configuration:
177	
178	<Config
179	  data={{
180	    palette: {
181	      "git-foreground": "#193549",
182	      git: "#FFFB38",
183	      "git-modified": "#FF9248",
184	      "git-diverged": "#FF4500",
185	      "git-ahead": "#B388FF",
186	      "git-behind": "#B388FF",
187	      red: "#FF0000",
188	      green: "#00FF00",
189	      blue: "#0000FF",
190	      white: "#FFFFFF",
191	      black: "#111111",
192	    },
193	  }}
194	/>
195	
196	Color names (palette keys) can have any string value, so be creative.
197	Color values, on the other hand, should adhere to the **Standard color** format, including `transparent`.
198	
199	Alternatively, a palette value can be a [template][templates] that resolves to any valid color value, including
200	a _Palette reference_. The template is rendered every time the color is resolved, so it can use any of the
201	[global template properties][template-properties] to switch colors based on context. For example, to use a
202	different color when a `Git` segment is active in the prompt:
203	
204	<Config
205	  data={{
206	    palette: {
207	      "separator-git": '{{ if .Segments.Contains "Git" }}p:bg0{{ else }}p:green{{ end }}',
208	    },
209	  }}
210	/>
211	
212	### Using a Palette
213	
214	You can now use _Palette references_ in any [Segment's][segment] `foreground`, `foreground_templates`,
215	`background`, `background_templates` properties, and other config properties that expect **Standard color** value.
216	_Palette reference_ format is `p:<palette key>`. Take a look at the [Git][git] segment using _Palette references_:
217	
218	<Config
219	  data={{
220	    type: "git",
221	    style: "powerline",
222	    powerline_symbol: "\uE0B0",
223	    foreground: "p:git-foreground",
224	    background: "p:git",
225	    background_templates: [
226	      "{{ if or (.Working.Changed) (.Staging.Changed) }}p:git-modified{{ end }}",
227	      "{{ if and (gt .Ahead 0) (gt .Behind 0) }}p:git-diverged{{ end }}",
228	      "{{ if gt .Ahead 0 }}p:git-ahead{{ end }}",
229	      "{{ if gt .Behind 0 }}p:git-behind{{ end }}",
230	    ],
231	  }}
232	/>
233	
234	Having all of the colors defined in one place allows you to import existing color configurations (usually with slight
235	tweaking to adhere to the format), easily change colors of multiple segments at once, and have a more
236	organized configuration overall. Be creative!
237	
238	### _Palette references_ and **Standard colors**
239	
240	Using Palette does not interfere with using **Standard colors** in your configuration. You can still use **Standard colors**
241	everywhere. This can be useful if you want to use a specific color for a single segment element, or in a
242	_Color override_ ([Battery segment][battery]):
243	
244	<Config
245	  data={{
246	    type: "battery",
247	    style: "powerline",
248	    invert_powerline: true,
249	    powerline_symbol: "\uE0B2",
250	    foreground: "p:white",
251	    background: "p:black",
252	    options: {
253	      discharging_icon: "<#ffa500>-</> ",
254	      charging_icon: "+ ",
255	      charged_icon: "* ",
256	    },
257	  }}
258	/>
259	
260	### Handling of invalid references
261	
262	Should you use an invalid _Palette reference_ as a color (for example typo `p:bleu` instead of `p:blue`),
263	the Palette engine will use the Transparent keyword as a fallback value. So if you see your prompt segments
264	rendered with incorrect colors, and you are using a Palette, be sure to check the correctness of your references.
265	
266	### Recursive resolution
267	
268	Palette allows for recursive _Palette reference_ resolution, up to 3 levels deep. You can use a _Palette
269	reference_ as a color value in Palette. This allows you to define named colors, and use references to those
270	colors as Palette values. A template value that resolves to a _Palette reference_ follows the same rules,
271	and the referenced value can itself be a template.
272	For example, `p:foreground` and `p:background` will be correctly set to "#CAF0F80" and "#023E8A":
273	
274	<Config
275	  data={{
276	    palette: {
277	      "light-blue": "#CAF0F8",
278	      "dark-blue": "#023E8A",
279	      foreground: "p:light-blue",
280	      background: "p:dark-blue",
281	    },
282	  }}
283	/>
284	
285	## Palettes
286	
287	If you want to use a `palette` conditionally, for example for **light or dark mode**, you can define multiple
288	`palettes` and a [template][templates] that resolves to the `palette` key. The `template` is evaluated at
289	runtime so your prompt can change at any time based on the outcome of the `template`.
290	
291	Take the following configuration:
292	
293	<Config
294	  data={{
295	    palettes: {
296	      template: '{{ if eq .Shell "pwsh" }}latte{{ else }}frappe{{ end }}',
297	      list: {
298	        latte: {
299	          black: "#262B44",
300	          green: "#59C9A5",
301	          orange: "#F07623",
302	          red: "#e64553",
303	          white: "#E0DEF4",
304	          yellow: "#df8e1d",
305	          blue: "#7287fd",
306	        },
307	        frappe: {
308	          black: "#262B44",
309	          green: "#59C9A5",
310	          orange: "#F07623",
311	          red: "#D81E5B",
312	          white: "#E0DEF4",
313	          yellow: "#F3AE35",
314	          blue: "#4B95E9",
315	        },
316	      },
317	    },
318	  }}
319	/>
320	
321	In this case, when the shell is `pwsh`, the `latte` palette will be used, otherwise it uses the `frappe` palette. If you want,
322	you could also add `frappe` as the default `palette`, given that one is used as a fallback when not match can be found based on what
323	the `template` resolves to. In case no match is available and no `palette` is defined, it will also fallback to `transparent`
324	for any palette color reference in templates/colors.
325	
326	If you want to avoid color duplication, you can use palettes in combination with the `palette` property. This way you can define
327	a color once and reuse it in multiple palettes. For example:
328	
329	<Config
330	  data={{
331	    palette: {
332	      black: "#262B44",
333	      green: "#59C9A5",
334	      orange: "#F07623",
335	    },
336	    palettes: {
337	      template: '{{ if eq .Shell "pwsh" }}latte{{ else }}frappe{{ end }}',
338	      list: {
339	        latte: {
340	          red: "#e64553",
341	          white: "#E0DEF4",
342	          yellow: "#df8e1d",
343	          blue: "#7287fd",
344	        },
345	        frappe: {
346	          red: "#D81E5B",
347	          white: "#E0DEF4",
348	          yellow: "#F3AE35",
349	          blue: "#4B95E9",
350	        },
351	      },
352	    },
353	  }}
354	/>
355	
356	:::info
357	If a color is defined in both palette and palettes, the palettes' resolved color will take precedence.
358	:::
359	
360	## Cycle
361	
362	When you want to display the same **sequence of colors** (background and foreground) regardless of which segments are active, you can
363	make use of the cycle property. This property is a list of colors which are used one after the other in a continuous loop. A defined
364	cycle always gets precedence over everything else.
365	
366	<Config
367	  data={{
368	    cycle: [
369	      {
370	        background: "p:blue",
371	        foreground: "p:white",
372	      },
373	      {
374	        background: "p:green",
375	        foreground: "p:black",
376	      },
377	      {
378	        background: "p:orange",
379	        foreground: "p:white",
380	      },
381	    ],
382	  }}
383	/>
384	
385	[hexcolors]: https://htmlcolorcodes.com/color-chart/material-design-color-chart/
386	[ansicolors]: https://htmlcolorcodes.com/color-chart/material-design-color-chart/
387	[git]: /docs/segments/scm/git
388	[battery]: /docs/segments/system/battery
389	[template-properties]: /docs/configuration/templates#global-properties
390	[aws]: /docs/segments/cloud/aws
391	[go-text-template]: https://golang.org/pkg/text/template/
392	[segment]: /docs/configuration/segment
393	[sprig]: https://masterminds.github.io/sprig/
394	[templates]: templates.mdx
395	[palette]: #palette
396

content.text:
Analysis done. Here's what's going on and options for the fix.

**Root cause:** `linear-gradient(a, b)` needs `minGradientCellsPerStop` (2) cells per stop — [engine.go:394](src/prompt/engine.go:394),[458](src/prompt/engine.go:458). 2 stops = 4 cells minimum, or `collapseGradient` flattens whole segment to solid last-stop color. Status segment text usually short, so any 2-stop gradient collapses.

**Proposed fix:** new gradient form that takes ONE base color and auto-derives stops by lightening/darkening it (HCL) — count of stops adapts to the segment's actual cell count at render time (`GradientCells` already receives `cells`), instead of being fixed by config. That sidesteps the width-collapse rule entirely since stop count is never fixed ahead of render.

Touches: [color/gradient.go](src/color/gradient.go) (new syntax + adaptive stop generation), [prompt/engine.go:458](src/prompt/engine.go:458) (bypass collapse for this form), docs/colors.mdx, tests.

Two design calls only you can make:

questions.question: Syntax for the single-color gradient keyword?

options.label: linear-gradient(#color) — single stop
options.description: Reuse existing linear-gradient(...), special-case: exactly one stop means auto-derive instead of error/collapse.

options.label: New function, e.g. shade(#color)
options.description: Separate keyword, keeps linear-gradient's 2+-stop contract untouched, self-documenting name.

questions.question: Shading algorithm — how should the single color spread across the segment?

options.label: Symmetric lighten/darken (Recommended)
options.description: Darker shade -> base color -> lighter shade (or vice versa), base color anchored at center. Reads as a sheen/glow.

options.label: Base to darker only
options.description: linear-gradient(color, darker-color) direction, base color anchored at the start edge.

options.label: Base to lighter only
options.description: linear-gradient(color, lighter-color) direction, base color anchored at the start edge.

result.output: Your questions have been answered: "Syntax for the single-color gradient keyword?"="linear-gradient(#color) — single stop", "Shading algorithm — how should the single color spread across the segment?"="Symmetric lighten/darken (Recommended)". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

900	func LuvWhiteRef(l, u, v float64, wref [3]float64) Color {
901		return Xyz(LuvToXyzWhiteRef(l, u, v, wref))
902	}
903	
904	// DistanceLuv is a good measure of visual similarity between two colors!
905	// A result of 0 would mean identical colors, while a result of 1 or higher
906	// means the colors differ a lot.
907	func (c1 Color) DistanceLuv(c2 Color) float64 {
908		l1, u1, v1 := c1.Luv()
909		l2, u2, v2 := c2.Luv()
910		return math.Sqrt(sq(l1-l2) + sq(u1-u2) + sq(v1-v2))
911	}
912	
913	// BlendLuv blends two colors in the CIE-L*u*v* color-space, which should result in a smoother blend.
914	// t == 0 results in c1, t == 1 results in c2
915	func (c1 Color) BlendLuv(c2 Color, t float64) Color {
916		l1, u1, v1 := c1.Luv()
917		l2, u2, v2 := c2.Luv()
918		return Luv(l1+t*(l2-l1),
919			u1+t*(u2-u1),
920			v1+t*(v2-v1))
921	}
922	
923	/// HCL ///
924	///////////
925	// HCL is nothing else than L*a*b* in cylindrical coordinates!
926	// (this was wrong on English wikipedia, I fixed it, let's hope the fix stays.)
927	// But it is widely popular since it is a "correct HSV"
928	// http://www.hunterlab.com/appnotes/an09_96a.pdf
929	
930	// Converts the given color to HCL space using D65 as reference white.
931	// H values are in [0..360], C and L values are in [0..1] although C can overshoot 1.0
932	func (col Color) Hcl() (h, c, l float64) {
933		return col.HclWhiteRef(D65)
934	}
935	
936	func LabToHcl(L, a, b float64) (h, c, l float64) {
937		// Oops, floating point workaround necessary if a ~= b and both are very small (i.e. almost zero).
938		if math.Abs(b-a) > 1e-4 && math.Abs(a) > 1e-4 {
939			h = math.Mod(57.29577951308232087721*math.Atan2(b, a)+360.0, 360.0) // Rad2Deg
940		} else {
941			h = 0.0
942		}
943		c = math.Sqrt(sq(a) + sq(b))
944		l = L
945		return
946	}
947	
948	// Converts the given color to HCL space, taking into account
949	// a given reference white. (i.e. the monitor's white)
950	// H values are in [0..360], C and L values are in [0..1]
951	func (col Color) HclWhiteRef(wref [3]float64) (h, c, l float64) {
952		L, a, b := col.LabWhiteRef(wref)
953		return LabToHcl(L, a, b)
954	}
955	
956	// Generates a color by using data given in HCL space using D65 as reference white.
957	// H values are in [0..360], C and L values are in [0..1]
958	// WARNING: many combinations of `h`, `c`, and `l` values do not have corresponding
959	// valid RGB values, check the FAQ in the README if you're unsure.
960	func Hcl(h, c, l float64) Color {
961		return HclWhiteRef(h, c, l, D65)
962	}
963	
964	func HclToLab(h, c, l float64) (L, a, b float64) {
965		H := 0.01745329251994329576 * h // Deg2Rad
966		a = c * math.Cos(H)
967		b = c * math.Sin(H)
968		L = l
969		return
970	}
971	
972	// Generates a color by using data given in HCL space, taking
973	// into account a given reference white. (i.e. the monitor's white)
974	// H values are in [0..360], C and L values are in [0..1]
975	func HclWhiteRef(h, c, l float64, wref [3]float64) Color {
976		L, a, b := HclToLab(h, c, l)
977		return LabWhiteRef(L, a, b, wref)
978	}
979	
980	// BlendHcl blends two colors in the CIE-L*C*h° color-space, which should result in a smoother blend.
981	// t == 0 results in c1, t == 1 results in c2
982	func (col1 Color) BlendHcl(col2 Color, t float64) Color {
983		h1, c1, l1 := col1.Hcl()
984		h2, c2, l2 := col2.Hcl()
985	
986		// https://github.com/lucasb-eyer/go-colorful/pull/60
987		if c1 <= 0.00015 && c2 >= 0.00015 {
988			h1 = h2
989		} else if c2 <= 0.00015 && c1 >= 0.00015 {
990			h2 = h1
991		}
992	
993		// We know that h are both in [0..360]
994		return Hcl(interp_angle(h1, h2, t), c1+t*(c2-c1), l1+t*(l2-l1)).Clamped()
995	}
996	
997	// LuvLch
998	
999	// Converts the given color to LuvLCh space using D65 as reference white.

1	# Phase 2 — Plan
2	
3	Turn the approved analysis into an executable plan. The quality bar: an implementer-tier model
4	must be able to execute each task without asking questions.
5	
6	## Pin the spec
7	
8	Write the spec down before delegating anything. It contains:
9	
10	- The decided approach — including decisions already made, so the implementer does not relitigate
11	  them.
12	- Files to touch, and the entry points to start from.
13	- Constraints: style rules, patterns to follow (point at existing code), performance or
14	  compatibility requirements.
15	- Verification commands the implementer must run locally (build, tests, lint).
16	- Explicit non-goals: what the task must NOT change. This is what keeps subagents from wandering.
17	
18	## Split into tasks
19	
20	- One task = one self-contained unit an implementer can finish and verify on its own.
21	- Mark which tasks are independent and which consume another task's output. Parallelize the
22	  independent ones; sequence the rest. When in doubt, sequence — a merge conflict between two
23	  parallel subagents costs more than the parallelism saves.
24	- Documentation updates belong to the task that changes the behavior, not to a separate task.
25	
26	## Decide the workspace per task
27	
28	- Main working tree: when the task depends on uncommitted local changes, or when you will review
29	  and commit the result in the current session.
30	- Isolated worktree: everything else, especially parallel tasks — they must never share a
31	  working tree.
32	
33	## Output of this phase
34	
35	A task list where each entry names its executor tier (see Phase 3), its workspace, its
36	dependencies, and carries its pinned spec.
37

10	func TestIsGradient(t *testing.T) {
11		cases := []struct {
12			Case     string
13			Color    Ansi
14			Expected bool
15		}{
16			{Case: "gradient", Color: "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF)", Expected: true},
17			{Case: "hex", Color: "#FF0000", Expected: false},
18			{Case: "empty", Color: "", Expected: false},
19			{Case: "keyword", Color: Background, Expected: false},
20		}
21	
22		for _, tc := range cases {
23			assert.Equal(t, tc.Expected, tc.Color.IsGradient(), tc.Case)
24		}
25	}
26	
27	func TestGradientStops(t *testing.T) {
28		cases := []struct {
29			Case     string
30			Color    Ansi
31			Expected []Ansi
32		}{
33			{Case: "two stops", Color: "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF)", Expected: []Ansi{"#FF0000", "#0000FF"}},
34			{Case: "three stops", Color: "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #00FF00, #0000FF)", Expected: []Ansi{"#FF0000", "#00FF00", "#0000FF"}},
35			{Case: "whitespace variants", Color: "linear-gradient( #FF0000  ,  #0000FF )", Expected: []Ansi{"#FF0000", "#0000FF"}},
36			{Case: "palette ref stops", Color: "linear-gradient(p:red, p:blue)", Expected: []Ansi{"p:red", "p:blue"}},
37			{Case: "not a gradient", Color: "#FF0000", Expected: nil},
38			{Case: "empty string", Color: "", Expected: nil},
39			{Case: "no closing paren", Color: "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF", Expected: nil},
40			{Case: "nested gradient", Color: "linear-gradient(linear-gradient(#FF0000, #00FF00), #0000FF)", Expected: nil},
41			{Case: "empty stop between commas", Color: "linear-gradient(#FF0000,,#0000FF)", Expected: nil},
42			{Case: "trailing comma", Color: "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF,)", Expected: nil},
43		}
44	
45		for _, tc := range cases {
46			assert.Equal(t, tc.Expected, tc.Color.GradientStops(), tc.Case)
47		}
48	}
49	
50	func TestGradientFirstAndLast(t *testing.T) {
51		cases := []struct {
52			Case          string
53			Color         Ansi
54			ExpectedFirst Ansi
55			ExpectedLast  Ansi
56		}{
57			{Case: "two stops", Color: "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF)", ExpectedFirst: "#FF0000", ExpectedLast: "#0000FF"},
58			{Case: "three stops", Color: "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #00FF00, #0000FF)", ExpectedFirst: "#FF0000", ExpectedLast: "#0000FF"},

173	// TestGradientCellsKeywordStops verifies keyword stops resolve against the segment
174	// context before interpolation: parentBackground picks up the parent's color (a parent
175	// gradient collapses to its last stop), and keywords without a hex resolution
176	// invalidate the stop.
177	func TestGradientCellsKeywordStops(t *testing.T) {
178		parents := []*Set{{Background: "#112233", Foreground: "#445566"}}
179		gradientParents := []*Set{{Background: "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF)", Foreground: "#445566"}}
180		current := &Set{Background: "#778899", Foreground: "#AABBCC"}
181	
182		cases := []struct {
183			Current  *Set
184			Case     string
185			Color    Ansi
186			Expected Ansi
187			Parents  []*Set
188		}{
189			{
190				Case:     "parentBackground stop resolves to parent color",
191				Color:    "linear-gradient(parentBackground, #FFFFFF)",
192				Parents:  parents,
193				Expected: "38;2;17;34;51",
194			},
195			{
196				Case:     "parentBackground stop collapses a parent gradient to its last stop",
197				Color:    "linear-gradient(parentBackground, #FFFFFF)",
198				Parents:  gradientParents,
199				Expected: "38;2;0;0;255",
200			},
201			{
202				Case:     "foreground stop resolves to the current foreground",
203				Color:    "linear-gradient(foreground, #000000)",
204				Current:  current,
205				Expected: "38;2;170;187;204",
206			},
207		}
208	
209		for _, tc := range cases {
210			result := GradientCells(tc.Color, 2, &Defaults{}, false, tc.Current, tc.Parents)
211			assert.Len(t, result, 2, tc.Case)
212			if len(result) == 2 {
213				assert.Equal(t, tc.Expected, result[0], tc.Case)
214			}
215		}
216	
217		// a keyword with no context resolves to transparent, not a hex color
218		assert.Nil(t, GradientCells("linear-gradient(parentBackground, #FFFFFF)", 2, &Defaults{}, false, nil, nil), "keyword without context invalidates the stop")
219		assert.Nil(t, GradientCells("linear-gradient(transparent, #FFFFFF)", 2, &Defaults{}, false, current, parents), "transparent is never a valid stop")
220	}
221	
222	// TestGradientCellsAccentStop verifies the accent keyword works as a stop: it resolves
223	// through ToAnsi to a truecolor payload, which parseTrueColor recovers. An unresolved
224	// accent (empty Set) invalidates the stop instead of erroring hard.
225	func TestGradientCellsAccentStop(t *testing.T) {
226		resolver := &Defaults{
227			accent: &Set{
228				Foreground: "38;2;0;120;215",
229				Background: "48;2;0;120;215",
230			},
231		}
232	
233		result := GradientCells("linear-gradient(accent, #FFFFFF)", 2, resolver, false, nil, nil)
234		assert.Len(t, result, 2)
235		if len(result) == 2 {
236			assert.Equal(t, Ansi("38;2;0;120;215"), result[0], "first cell must be the accent color")
237			assert.Equal(t, Ansi("38;2;255;255;255"), result[1])
238		}
239	
240		assert.Nil(t, GradientCells("linear-gradient(accent, #FFFFFF)", 2, &Defaults{}, false, nil, nil), "unresolved accent invalidates the stop")
241	}
242	
243	func TestParseTrueColor(t *testing.T) {
244		cases := []struct {
245			Case  string
246			Input Ansi
247			OK    bool
248		}{
249			{Case: "foreground payload", Input: "38;2;255;0;128", OK: true},
250			{Case: "background payload", Input: "48;2;0;120;215", OK: true},
251			{Case: "256-color payload", Input: "38;5;146", OK: false},
252			{Case: "hex string", Input: "#FFFFFF", OK: false},
253			{Case: "out of range", Input: "38;2;300;0;0", OK: false},
254			{Case: "empty", Input: "", OK: false},
255		}
256	
257		for _, tc := range cases {
258			_, ok := parseTrueColor(tc.Input)
259			assert.Equal(t, tc.OK, ok, tc.Case)
260		}

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\markdown

# Markdown Content Rules

The following markdown content rules are enforced in the validators:

1. Headings: Use appropriate heading levels (H2, H3, etc.) to structure your content.
  Do not use an H1 heading, as this will be generated based on the title.
2. Lists: Use bullet points or numbered lists for lists. Ensure proper
  indentation and spacing.
3. Code Blocks: Use fenced code blocks for code snippets. Specify the language
  for syntax highlighting.
4. Links: Use proper markdown syntax for links. Ensure that links are valid and
  accessible.
5. Images: Use proper markdown syntax for images. Include alt text for
  accessibility.
6. Tables: Use markdown tables for tabular data. Ensure proper formatting and
  alignment.
7. Line Length: Limit line length to 120 characters for readability.
8. Whitespace: Use appropriate whitespace to separate sections and improve readability.
9. Front Matter: Include YAML front matter at the beginning of the file with required metadata fields.

## Formatting and Structure

Follow these guidelines for formatting and structuring your markdown content:

- Headings: Use `##` for H2 and `###` for H3. Ensure that headings are used in a
  hierarchical manner. Recommend restructuring if content includes H4, and more
  strongly recommend for H5.
- Lists: Use `-` for bullet points and `1.` for numbered lists. Indent nested lists with two spaces.
- Code Blocks: Use fenced code blocks with a language for syntax highlighting:

  ```go
  fmt.Println("hello")
  ```

- Links:
  - Inline: `[Docs](https://example.com/docs)`
  - Reference-style: `[Docs][docs]` and add at the end of the page:
    `[docs]: https://example.com/docs`
- Images: Use `![alt text](image URL)` for images. Include a brief description
  of the image in the alt text.
- Tables: Use `|` to create tables. Ensure that columns are properly aligned
  and headers are included.
- Line Length: Break lines at 120 characters to improve readability. Use soft
  line breaks for long paragraphs.
- Whitespace: Use blank lines to separate sections and improve readability. Avoid excessive whitespace.

## Post-Edit Verification

After editing any `.md` or `.mdx` file, run the following command and resolve all reported
errors before considering the task complete:

```shell
npx markdownlint-cli2 <edited-file>
```

This closes the feedback loop so violations are caught and fixed immediately rather than
relying on an external CI run.

## Validation Requirements

Ensure compliance with the following validation requirements:

- Content Rules: Ensure that the content follows the markdown content rules
  specified above.
- Formatting: Ensure that the content is properly formatted and structured
  according to the guidelines.
- Validation: Run the validation tools to check for compliance with the rules
  and guidelines.

input.old_string: ### Supported stops
input.new_string:
### Auto-shade from a single color

A gradient with a single stop, `linear-gradient(#3465A4)`, spreads that one color into a
darker-to-lighter blend sized to the segment's visible width. Use it when a segment is too narrow
for a real two-stop gradient to render as anything but a solid color, see
[Stops and segment width](#stops-and-segment-width).

<Config
  data={{
    type: "text",
    style: "plain",
    background: "linear-gradient(#3465A4)",
  }}
/>

The configured color always lands in the middle of the blend. A single-cell segment renders it
unmodified instead of a shaded endpoint.

### Supported stops

93	A `background` or `foreground` keyword in a [color override](#color-overrides) inside a gradient
94	segment resolves to the gradient's color at that position in the text: a leading
95	`<background,transparent>` cap picks up the first stop, a trailing one the color where the
96	gradient ends, so template caps stay seamless without hardcoding edge colors.
97	
98	A gradient without at least two supported stops renders as a solid color: the last stop goes through
99	the regular color pipeline, so `linear-gradient(red, blue)` renders solid blue rather than failing —
100	matching the color its separators and width-collapsed form already use. Rejected stops are reported
101	in `oh-my-posh debug` output.

input.new_string:
A gradient with two or more configured stops that resolves to fewer than two supported colors
renders as a solid color: the last stop goes through the regular color pipeline, so
`linear-gradient(red, blue)` renders solid blue rather than failing — matching the color its
separators and width-collapsed form already use. Rejected stops are reported in `oh-my-posh debug`
output. A single _configured_ stop is not this fallback case, it's the
[auto-shade](#auto-shade-from-a-single-color) form.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\conventional-commit

# Conventional Commit

## Commit Message Structure

```text
<type>(<scope>): <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

### Types

| Type       | When to use                                            |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `feat`     | A new feature                                          |
| `fix`      | A bug fix                                              |
| `docs`     | Documentation changes, no code                         |
| `style`    | Formatting, missing semicolons, etc. (no logic change) |
| `refactor` | Code change that is neither a fix nor a feature        |
| `perf`     | Performance improvement                                |
| `test`     | Adding or correcting tests                             |
| `ci`       | CI configuration changes                               |
| `chore`    | Maintenance tasks (updating deps, tooling, etc.)       |
| `revert`   | Reverts a previous commit                              |

Append `!` after the type/scope to signal a **breaking change**: `feat!:` or `feat(api)!:`
When a change breaks existing behavior, **both markers are mandatory**: the `!` suffix on the type
**and** the `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. They always appear together — never one without the other.

### Scope

Optional. Use the name of the area affected, e.g., `segment`, `cache`, `config`, `ui`.
Omit when the change is truly cross-cutting.

### Description

- Required. One short imperative sentence, no period at the end. The full header line (type +
  scope + description) must be **72 characters or fewer**. Aim for **50 characters or fewer for
  the description itself** — this almost always keeps the full header within budget regardless
  of type and scope length.
- Use the imperative mood: "add", not "added" or "adds". Never past tense or present-third-person:
  ✗ `added`, `fixed`, `bumped`, `implemented` → ✓ `add`, `fix`, `bump`, `implement`.
- **Never mirror the input's phrasing.** If the request uses past-tense words (`updated`, `added`,
  `bumped`, `was removed`, `got regenerated`), convert them to imperative before writing the
  description: `update`, `add`, `bump`, `remove`, `regenerate`.

### Body

Optional. Add context about _why_ the change was made, not _what_. The diff shows that.
Wrap at 72 characters.

### Footer

Use for:

- `BREAKING CHANGE: <description>` (required when `!` is used; explains the break).
- Issue references: `Closes #123`, `Fixes #456`.
- Co-authors: `Co-Authored-By: Name <email>`.

## Workflow

1. Run `git status` to review changed files.
2. Run `git diff` and `git diff --cached` to inspect staged and unstaged changes.
3. Identify the **type** from the table above. Ask yourself: does this change **remove, rename, or
   alter existing behavior** that callers depend on? If yes → it is a breaking change: use `!`
   after the type/scope **and** add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. Both markers are always required
   together.
4. Identify the **scope** from the files/area changed.
5. Write a short **description** in the imperative mood.
6. Add a **body** if the _why_ needs explanation.
7. Add a **footer** for breaking changes or issue references.
8. Stage the relevant files explicitly (avoid `git add -A`).
9. Commit with a message that preserves multi-line formatting when body/footer are present.

## Examples

```text
feat(segment): add Ramadan segment with Aladhan API
fix(cache): always store mod time
docs(readme): update installation instructions
refactor(config): simplify option parsing logic
chore(deps): bump github.com/shirou/gopsutil/v4
feat(segment)!: rename template property StartTime to Start

BREAKING CHANGE: template strings using .StartTime must be updated to .Start
```

## Validation Checklist

- [ ] Type is one of the allowed values in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] The commit message respects the rules defined in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] Scope (if present) reflects the actual area changed
- [ ] Description is imperative mood, no trailing period
- [ ] Full header line (type + scope + description) is 72 characters or fewer
- [ ] Both `!` after type/scope **and** `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer are present whenever the change breaks existing behavior
- [ ] No sensitive files staged (.env, credentials, etc.)

content.text:
Done. Committed as `e85d6f03`.

`linear-gradient(#color)` with one stop now auto-shades: darker to base to lighter, spread across whatever cells the segment has. No config-level cap change needed — bypass already existed since single-stop gradients skip the `minGradientCellsPerStop` collapse in [engine.go:464](src/prompt/engine.go:464).

For status segment: swap two-color gradient for `linear-gradient(#yourcolor)`.

Not pushed — say the word if you want it up.
The transition seems to be a bit too strong if you look at smaller segments.

Confirmed root cause: at 2–3 cells, interpolation jumps straight from the darker endpoint to the lighter one — the base color in the middle never actually renders, so the segment shows the *full* 2×delta contrast with no smoothing, not just a coarser version of it. Wider segments smooth that same range across more cells so it reads subtler.

content.text:
Found it. `GradientLast()` — used by powerline separators, diamond caps, and parent-color inheritance to color the segment's trailing edge — doesn't know about the auto-shade expansion; it still returns the flat, unshaded base color. So the body gradient (darker → base → lighter, base only ever landing on the exact middle cell) ends on "lighter," but the separator glyph right after it switches back to the flat base color — that's the "dark again" seam. And with base anchored only at dead center, it rarely lines up with an actual cell at typical widths anyway.

Redesigning: anchor the gradient at the *exact configured color* (start) and shade toward one fixed darker endpoint (end) — a real two-stop shape reusing the existing, already-correct 2-stop machinery, and fixing `GradientLast()` so edges match the body exactly. Cell count no longer needs artificial scaling — more cells naturally means finer steps between the same two fixed endpoints, which is exactly the existing gradient behavior everywhere else.

173	// autoShadeDelta is the HCL blend fraction used to derive the darker/lighter endpoints
174	// for a single-stop (auto-shade) gradient, once the segment is wide enough to show the
175	// base color between them (autoShadeFullCells or more visible cells).
176	const autoShadeDelta = 0.15
177	
178	// autoShadeFullCells is the cell count at which autoShade reaches its full delta. Below
179	// it, the interpolation in GradientCells has no room for the base color to render between
180	// the two endpoints (at 2 cells they sit directly adjacent), so the whole delta reads as
181	// one hard step; scaling it down by cells keeps narrow segments looking like a subtle
182	// sheen instead of a sharp two-tone split.
183	const autoShadeFullCells = 6
184	
185	var (
186		hclBlack = colorful.Color{R: 0, G: 0, B: 0}
187		hclWhite = colorful.Color{R: 1, G: 1, B: 1}
188	)
189	
190	// autoShade spreads a single resolved color into a darker-base-lighter blend in HCL
191	// space, giving a single-stop gradient the same darker/lighter/base endpoints
192	// GradientCells already knows how to interpolate across cells. cells must be >= 2.
193	func autoShade(base colorful.Color, cells int) []colorful.Color {
194		delta := autoShadeDelta
195		if cells < autoShadeFullCells {
196			delta *= float64(cells) / float64(autoShadeFullCells)
197		}
198	
199		darker := base.BlendHcl(hclBlack, delta).Clamped()
200		lighter := base.BlendHcl(hclWhite, delta).Clamped()
201	
202		return []colorful.Color{darker, base, lighter}
203	}

119			}
120	
121			// the OS accent color only resolves in ToAnsi, to a truecolor payload
122			// rather than hex; parseTrueColor recovers the RGB triplet from it.
123			if resolved == Accent {
124				resolved = resolver.ToAnsi(Accent, false)
125			}
126	
127			clr, err := colorful.Hex(resolved.String())
128			if err != nil {
129				var ok bool
130				if clr, ok = parseTrueColor(resolved); !ok {
131					log.Errorf("gradient %s: stop %s does not resolve to a color, only hex colors, palette references, and keywords resolving to a color can be interpolated", c, stop)
132					continue
133				}
134			}
135	
136			colors = append(colors, clr)
137		}
138	
139		// a single explicit stop, e.g. linear-gradient(#3465a4), is an auto-shade request:
140		// turn the one resolved color into a real two-stop gradient running from that exact
141		// color to a fixed darker shade of it, reusing the interpolation below unchanged. The
142		// segment's own cell count (fewer cells = coarser steps) already gives narrow segments
143		// a gentler ramp, same as any other gradient; see GradientLast for the matching edge.
144		if len(stops) == 1 {
145			if len(colors) == 0 {
146				log.Errorf("gradient %s: stop does not resolve to a color", c)
147				return nil
148			}
149	
150			colors = []colorful.Color{colors[0], autoShade(colors[0])}
151		}
152	
153		if len(colors) < 2 {
154			log.Errorf("gradient %s: needs at least two valid stops, rendering the last stop as a solid color", c)
155			return nil
156		}
157	
158		if cached, ok := gradientCellCache[gradientKey(colors, cells, isBackground)]; ok {
159			return cached
160		}
161	
162		if cells == 1 {
163			return cacheGradientCells(colors, cells, isBackground, []Ansi{ansiFromColorful(colors[0], isBackground)})
164		}
165	
166		segments := len(colors) - 1
167		result := make([]Ansi, cells)
168	
169		for i := range cells {
170			position := float64(i) / float64(cells-1) * float64(segments)
171	
172			segment := int(position)
173			if segment >= segments {
174				segment = segments - 1
175			}
176	
177			blended := colors[segment].BlendHcl(colors[segment+1], position-float64(segment)).Clamped()
178			result[i] = ansiFromColorful(blended, isBackground)
179		}
180	
181		return cacheGradientCells(colors, cells, isBackground, result)
182	}
183	
184	// autoShadeDelta is the HCL blend-toward-black fraction used to derive a single-stop
185	// (auto-shade) gradient's second stop. It is fixed regardless of segment width: the
186	// interpolation in GradientCells already spreads a fixed delta into more, smaller steps
187	// on a wide segment and fewer, larger ones on a narrow one, same as any other gradient.
188	const autoShadeDelta = 0.15
189	
190	var hclBlack = colorful.Color{R: 0, G: 0, B: 0}
191	
192	// autoShade darkens base by autoShadeDelta in HCL space, giving a single-stop gradient
193	// its second stop. GradientLast mirrors this on the raw (unresolved) stop text so a
194	// segment's trailing separator/cap matches the last color GradientCells renders.
195	func autoShade(base colorful.Color) colorful.Color {
196		return base.BlendHcl(hclBlack, autoShadeDelta).Clamped()
197	}
198	
199	// gradientCellCache memoizes interpolation results keyed on the RESOLVED stop colors
200	// (keyword and palette stops resolve before the key is built, so context changes miss
201	// the cache correctly), the cell count, the channel, and the TrueColor mode. Prompt
202	// rendering is single-threaded (see the terminal writer's package state), so a plain
203	// map suffices. Bounded to keep long-lived daemons from growing it unchecked.
204	var gradientCellCache = make(map[string][]Ansi)
205	
206	const gradientCellCacheLimit = 128
207	
208	func gradientKey(colors []colorful.Color, cells int, isBackground bool) string {
209		buf := make([]byte, 0, 8+len(colors)*12)
210	
211		for _, clr := range colors {
212			r, g, b := clr.RGB255()
213			buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(r), 10)
214			buf = append(buf, ';')
215			buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(g), 10)
216			buf = append(buf, ';')
217			buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(b), 10)
218			buf = append(buf, ',')
219		}
220	
221		buf = strconv.AppendInt(buf, int64(cells), 10)
222	
223		if isBackground {
224			buf = append(buf, 'b')
225		}
226	
227		if TrueColor {
228			buf = append(buf, 't')
229		}
230	
231		return string(buf)
232	}
233	
234	func cacheGradientCells(colors []colorful.Color, cells int, isBackground bool, result []Ansi) []Ansi {
235		if len(gradientCellCache) >= gradientCellCacheLimit {
236			gradientCellCache = make(map[string][]Ansi)
237		}
238	
239		gradientCellCache[gradientKey(colors, cells, isBackground)] = result
240		return result
241	}
242	
243	// parseTrueColor parses a truecolor ANSI payload ("38;2;r;g;b" or "48;2;r;g;b") back into
244	// a colorful.Color. The OS accent color resolves to this form instead of hex.
245	func parseTrueColor(c Ansi) (colorful.Color, bool) {
246		parts := strings.Split(c.String(), ";")
247		if len(parts) != 5 || (parts[0] != "38" && parts[0] != "48") || parts[1] != "2" {
248			return colorful.Color{}, false
249		}
250	
251		rgb := make([]uint8, 3)
252		for i, part := range parts[2:] {
253			val, err := strconv.ParseUint(part, 10, 8)
254			if err != nil {
255				return colorful.Color{}, false
256			}
257	
258			rgb[i] = uint8(val)
259		}
260	
261		return colorful.Color{R: float64(rgb[0]) / 255.0, G: float64(rgb[1]) / 255.0, B: float64(rgb[2]) / 255.0}, true
262	}
263	
264	// ansiFromColorful converts an interpolated HCL color to a ready-to-print ANSI code, honoring
265	// the package-level TrueColor flag. The truecolor payload is built with strconv appends
266	// rather than gookit's fmt.Sprintf path: one allocation per cell instead of four, on what
267	// is the gradient hot path's dominant allocation site.
268	func ansiFromColorful(c colorful.Color, isBackground bool) Ansi {
269		r, g, b := c.RGB255()
270	
271		if !TrueColor {
272			return Ansi(color.RGB(r, g, b, isBackground).C256().String())
273		}
274	
275		buf := make([]byte, 0, 16)
276	
277		if isBackground {
278			buf = append(buf, "48;2;"...)
279		} else {
280			buf = append(buf, "38;2;"...)
281		}
282	
283		buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(r), 10)
284		buf = append(buf, ';')
285		buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(g), 10)
286		buf = append(buf, ';')
287		buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(b), 10)
288	
289		return Ansi(buf)
290	}

input.old_string:
### Auto-shade from a single color

A gradient with a single stop, `linear-gradient(#3465A4)`, spreads that one color into a
darker-to-lighter blend sized to the segment's visible width. Use it when a segment is too narrow
for a real two-stop gradient to render as anything but a solid color, see
[Stops and segment width](#stops-and-segment-width).

<Config
  data={{
    type: "text",
    style: "plain",
    background: "linear-gradient(#3465A4)",
  }}
/>

The configured color always lands in the middle of the blend. A single-cell segment renders it
unmodified instead of a shaded endpoint.
input.new_string:
### Auto-shade from a single color

A gradient with a single stop, `linear-gradient(#3465A4)`, runs from that exact color to a fixed
darker shade of it, letting one configured color produce a gradient effect on a segment too narrow
for a real two-stop gradient to render as anything but a solid color, see
[Stops and segment width](#stops-and-segment-width).

<Config
  data={{
    type: "text",
    style: "plain",
    background: "linear-gradient(#3465A4)",
  }}
/>

The first cell always renders the exact configured color; the last renders the darker shade.
Everything in between interpolates the same way a two-stop gradient does, so a wide segment shows
a gentler ramp and a narrow one a coarser one. A single-cell segment renders the configured color
unmodified.

git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(color): auto-shade single-stop gradients

A single-stop linear-gradient(#color) previously collapsed to a
useless solid fallback. Turn it into a real two-stop gradient
running from the exact configured color to a fixed darker HCL
shade, so a segment too narrow for a real two-stop gradient still
gets a gradient effect from one configured color.

GradientLast mirrors the same shading on the raw stop text, so
powerline separators, diamond caps, and parentBackground/
parentForeground line up with the color the body actually ends
on instead of jumping back to the unshaded configured color.
EOF
)"
git log --oneline -3

content.text: Squashed clean as `6fa85fd6`. Root cause was `GradientLast()` staying blind to the shading, so separators/caps flashed back to the flat base color right after the body — and the old symmetric darker-base-lighter shape hid the base color anywhere but dead center. Fixed by anchoring at the exact configured color and shading toward one fixed darker endpoint, reusing the existing two-stop machinery so edges and body always agree, and coarseness now naturally scales with segment width instead of a separate hack.

Still not absolutely perfect. Provide this information, including the screenshot, to Fable to do a detailed analysis on how we could solve this problem. Point Fable at the configuration that I have to show the status segment that is comfy. Make it do a correct proposal to fix how we could make a pretty gradient for all lengths when they only use one gradient color.

1	# Escalation triggers
2	
3	The coordinator owns every phase by default. Escalation is a bounded subagent call to the
4	strongest available model for one specific judgment call — not a handoff of the phase. The
5	coordinator frames the question, gets the answer, and stays the one accountable for the result.
6	
7	Escalate when one of these is actually true, not on a general feeling that the task is hard:
8	
9	- Root cause can't be pinned with confidence after actually reading the code — not after
10	  re-reading the report again.
11	- The change is architectural: it crosses a module boundary, touches a public API/interface, or
12	  introduces a cross-cutting abstraction.
13	- The code is security-, auth-, crypto-, payments-, or data-migration-sensitive.
14	- The operation is irreversible or high-blast-radius (schema migration, deletion, force-push,
15	  production config).
16	- An implementer has reported a spec gap or contradiction more than once on the same task.
17	- Reviewing a diff leaves the coordinator unsure whether the fix is correct or merely plausible.
18	- The user explicitly asked for a second opinion or an adversarial review.
19	
20	None of these fire on routine work. Most tasks should complete without ever calling Escalation
21	tier — the coordinator is capable enough to do the analysis, review, and verification itself, and
22	the strongest model gets paid for only on the calls that actually need it.
23	
24	## How to escalate
25	
26	Frame the specific question, not the whole task. Hand over the pinned context needed to answer
27	it — the relevant code, the hypothesis so far, why it's uncertain — get the answer, and resume
28	the phase. Escalation never becomes the new owner of the task, and it never decides scope; it
29	answers the question it was asked and returns control to the coordinator.
