input.description: Deep design analysis for single-color gradients
input.prompt:
You are being asked for a DESIGN ANALYSIS AND WRITTEN PROPOSAL ONLY. Do not write or edit any code — the coordinator (a separate Claude Code session) will implement whatever you recommend after reviewing it with the user. Repo: D:\oh-my-posh (Go, the oh-my-posh prompt engine). Read whatever source you need with your own tools; the paths below are a starting point, not the full picture.

## Background

oh-my-posh segments can have a background/foreground color of the form `linear-gradient(stop1, stop2, ...)`, interpolated per-character-cell across the segment's visible width (see src/color/gradient.go, GradientCells). A real 2+ stop gradient needs at least `minGradientCellsPerStop` (2) visible cells per stop or it collapses to a solid color (src/prompt/engine.go, collapseGradient, const minGradientCellsPerStop). Many segments (icons, short status indicators) are only 2-4 cells wide, too narrow for a real 2-stop gradient to look like anything but a flat block.

## What we built (3 iterations, user still not satisfied)

We added a special case: `linear-gradient(#singleColor)` — literally ONE stop — is treated as an "auto-shade" request instead of the normal 2+-stop syntax. It's implemented purely as a derived 2-color gradient (base color + one computed shade), reusing the existing per-cell linear-HCL-interpolation machinery unchanged.

Current implementation (src/color/gradient.go):
```go
const autoShadeDelta = 0.15
var hclBlack = colorful.Color{R: 0, G: 0, B: 0}

func autoShade(base colorful.Color) colorful.Color {
	return base.BlendHcl(hclBlack, autoShadeDelta).Clamped()
}
```
Inside `GradientCells`, when the raw config has exactly one stop: `colors = []colorful.Color{colors[0], autoShade(colors[0])}`, then it falls into the SAME per-cell interpolation loop every other 2-stop gradient uses (linear position along HCL-space BlendHcl across `cells` steps, first cell = position 0 = the base color exactly, last cell = position 1 = the shaded color exactly).

`GradientLast()` (used by powerline separators, diamond caps, and parentBackground/parentForeground inheritance to color a segment's trailing edge) was also patched to mirror this exact shading on the raw stop text, so the separator/cap after the segment matches the last rendered body cell instead of showing the flat unshaded color — that was iteration 2's bug (edges didn't match the body, produced a visible "jump" back to the base color right after the gradient).

Iteration 1 was a SYMMETRIC darker→base→lighter blend (base pinned to the exact middle position) — this made the base color invisible on most cell counts (it only lands on a cell when cells is odd) and, combined with the GradientLast bug above, produced a "dark, then light, then dark again" artifact the user could see clearly.

Iteration 3 (current, described above) fixed both of those: base is now always the literal first cell, shaded color is always the literal last cell, monotonic, and edges match the body exactly. The user says it is STILL not "absolutely perfect" on small segments, without further detail this time — they've asked us to get a fresh, independent, thorough analysis instead of continuing to guess.

## The exact real-world case that's still not right

The user's live config (~/.posh.omp.jsonc) has this segment (JSON, jsonc format):
```jsonc
{
  "options": {
    "always_enabled": true,
    "status_separator": " ",
    "status_template": "{{ if eq .Code 0 }}{{ else }}{{ end }}"
  },
  "leading_diamond": "<transparent,background></>",
  "trailing_diamond": "",
  "template": " {{ .String }} ",
  "foreground": "p:white",
  "background": "linear-gradient(p:teal)",
  "type": "status",
  "style": "diamond",
  "background_templates": [
    "{{ if .Error }}linear-gradient(p:maroon){{ end }}"
  ]
}
```
Palette: `"teal": "#179299"`. This is a Nerd Font powerline-style prompt segment: a leading diamond glyph (U+E0B0, an arrow/chevron shape, colored via a `<transparent,background>` override that picks up the gradient's first stop), then the template body ` {{ .String }} ` — a space, ONE icon glyph (a checkmark U+F00C on success), and a trailing space — so exactly 3 visible cells of actual per-cell gradient interpolation — then a trailing diamond glyph (U+E0B4, a rounded cap) that automatically picks up the gradient's LAST stop (via GradientLast, per the powerline/diamond-cap convention already built into the renderer).

Two screenshots were shared in chat (not available to you as files, described here): a small pill/badge-shaped segment — purple arrow from the previous segment, then a chevron leading into a teal body containing a white checkmark, then a rounded teal-ish trailing cap — going from a medium teal into a very slightly different, slightly darker/duller teal by the end. The user's complaint, verbatim across the two rounds of feedback: first round described the OLD (iteration-1) symmetric version as "jumps to dark straight away and then becomes lighter and then is a bit darker again" — i.e. non-monotonic, edges inconsistent with body. After we shipped iteration 3 (base-anchored, monotonic, edge-consistent), the user's latest feedback is just: "Still not absolutely perfect" — no further specifics — and asked us to get a second opinion from you rather than keep guessing.

For grounding, here's what iteration 3 actually renders today for this exact base color (`#179299`) at various cell counts (background/48;2;r;g;b codes, TrueColor on), computed directly from the current code:
```
1 cell:  [23,146,153]
2 cells: [23,146,153] [28,123,128]
3 cells (the real case above): [23,146,153] [26,134,141] [28,123,128]
4 cells: [23,146,153] [25,138,145] [27,130,137] [28,123,128]
6 cells: [23,146,153] [24,141,148] [25,137,143] [26,132,138] [27,127,133] [28,123,128]
12 cells: [23,146,153] [24,144,151] [24,142,148] [25,140,146] [25,137,144] [26,135,142] [26,133,139] [27,131,137] [27,129,135] [27,127,133] [27,125,131] [28,123,128]
```
`GradientLast()` = `#1c7b80` (28,123,128), `GradientFirst()` = `#179299` (23,146,153) — matching the endpoints above, confirming edge consistency is working as designed.

Note the total shift here is actually quite small in raw RGB terms (roughly a 16% drop in the green channel, base to last stop) — so "too strong" may not be the (only) remaining problem; it could be about smoothness/perceived banding at only 3 discrete steps, the icon glyph sitting on an odd blended cell, the direction/hue of the shade, non-linear perceptual spacing, or something about how HCL-space blend-toward-black behaves on a saturated teal specifically. We genuinely don't know which — that's what we need your analysis for.

## Your task

1. Read src/color/gradient.go in full (GradientCells, GradientFirst/Last, autoShade, the per-cell BlendHcl interpolation loop) and src/prompt/engine.go's collapseGradient/minGradientCellsPerStop, to understand the existing constraints exactly.
2. Diagnose, as precisely as you can from the data above, what is most likely still visually wrong about a 1-color auto-shaded gradient at very narrow widths (2-4 cells) like this real case, and why iteration 3's fix didn't fully solve it. Consider things like: perceptual (non-linear) lightness spacing vs. raw HCL L; whether darkening a saturated teal toward pure black is the right hue-preserving move vs. some other approach (e.g. blending toward a desaturated/deeper version of the same hue, or adjusting chroma too); whether a single fixed direction (always darken) is the right default vs. adapting direction to make the shade read as intentional rather than "slightly off"; whether the discrete-step banding itself (independent of the specific colors chosen) is inherently the problem at 2-4 cells and needs a fundamentally different treatment (e.g. dithering, a non-linear easing curve, or accepting a stronger delta on purpose specifically when cells is very low so the intent reads clearly as "a gradient" rather than "did the color change or is that a rendering glitch").
3. Propose a CONCRETE fix: exact algorithm/formula (color space, direction, delta or delta curve as a function of cell count, any easing), how it behaves at 1 cell, 2 cells, 3 cells (this exact case), and 10+ cells, and how GradientLast should be computed to stay consistent with whatever you propose for the body. Prefer solutions that don't need new config surface (no new keywords/properties) unless you think that's genuinely necessary — if you do think a new keyword or config option is warranted, say so explicitly and explain why the existing single-stop `linear-gradient(#color)` syntax isn't enough.
4. Flag any tradeoffs or things you're unsure about rather than presenting one option as certain.

Report back a written technical proposal (prose + the concrete formula/pseudocode), not a code diff. Keep it focused — this is one specific design question, not a general code review.

content.text:
Fable's diagnosis, terse:

**Real bug found — matches your exact config.** Your segment uses `linear-gradient(p:teal)`, a palette reference, not a literal hex. `GradientLast()` tries `colorful.Hex("p:teal")` — fails, since it's not hex — falls back to the raw unshaded `p:teal`. Body cells resolve+shade fine (`#179299` → `#1c7b80` last cell), but the trailing rounded cap resolves straight to `#179299`, the bright unshaded base. Body darkens, then the cap pops back bright. Same hole for `parentBackground`/`parentForeground` chains. Root cause: palette refs never got wired through the resolve-then-shade path I built — only literal hex stops work. This alone likely explains "still not perfect."

**Second, smaller issue:** current formula blends toward black in HCL, which drags chroma down too — looks muddy/desaturated rather than a clean deep shade. Fable's fix: darken lightness only, preserve hue+chroma (walk chroma down only as far as gamut forces), clamped proportional delta so dark bases still show *something* and bright ones don't overshoot.

**Proposal:**
- Part A (fix the bug): resolve palette refs inside gradient strings once, upfront in `engine.go`'s `resolvePaletteReference`, before anything downstream runs — `linear-gradient(p:teal)` becomes `linear-gradient(#179299)` early, so `GradientLast`, separators, diamond caps all see real hex.
- Part B (nicer shade): swap blend-toward-black for hue/chroma-preserving lightness drop.

For your teal: current last-stop `#1c7b80` (muted) → proposed `#04777d` (deeper, same hue).

Want both, or just Part A first to see if that alone fixes it?
do both

index ef60a3b2..bc165bfe 100644
--- a/src/color/gradient.go
+++ b/src/color/gradient.go
@@ -1,6 +1,7 @@
 package color
 
 import (
+	"math"
 	"strconv"
 	"strings"
 
@@ -181,19 +182,39 @@ func GradientCells(c Ansi, cells int, resolver String, isBackground bool, curren
 	return cacheGradientCells(colors, cells, isBackground, result)
 }
 
-// autoShadeDelta is the HCL blend-toward-black fraction used to derive a single-stop
-// (auto-shade) gradient's second stop. It is fixed regardless of segment width: the
-// interpolation in GradientCells already spreads a fixed delta into more, smaller steps
-// on a wide segment and fewer, larger ones on a narrow one, same as any other gradient.
-const autoShadeDelta = 0.15
-
-var hclBlack = colorful.Color{R: 0, G: 0, B: 0}
+// autoShadeDeltaSlope/Floor/Ceiling shape the lightness drop autoShade applies, as a
+// fraction of the base color's own HCL lightness: proportional (so a near-black base
+// still shifts visibly and a near-white one doesn't overshoot), clamped to a floor (a
+// visible ramp even on already-dark colors) and a ceiling (no harsh drop on light ones).
+const (
+	autoShadeDeltaSlope   = 0.18
+	autoShadeDeltaFloor   = 0.06
+	autoShadeDeltaCeiling = 0.12
+	autoShadeMinLightness = 0.02
+	autoShadeChromaStep   = 0.005
+)
 
-// autoShade darkens base by autoShadeDelta in HCL space, giving a single-stop gradient
-// its second stop. GradientLast mirrors this on the raw (unresolved) stop text so a
-// segment's trailing separator/cap matches the last color GradientCells renders.
+// autoShade derives a single-stop (auto-shade) gradient's second stop: same hue, same
+// chroma other than what the sRGB gamut forces away, lightness dropped by a clamped
+// fraction of the base's own lightness. Blending toward black (the previous approach)
+// pulls chroma down linearly alongside lightness, which reads as the color going muddy
+// rather than deepening; walking chroma down only as far as IsValid demands keeps the
+// hue exact and preserves as much saturation as the gamut allows at the new lightness.
+// GradientLast mirrors this on the raw (unresolved) stop text so a segment's trailing
+// separator/cap matches the color GradientCells renders the last cell as.
 func autoShade(base colorful.Color) colorful.Color {
-	return base.BlendHcl(hclBlack, autoShadeDelta).Clamped()
+	h, c, l := base.Hcl()
+
+	delta := math.Max(autoShadeDeltaFloor, math.Min(autoShadeDeltaCeiling, autoShadeDeltaSlope*l))
+	l = math.Max(autoShadeMinLightness, l-delta)
+
+	shade := colorful.Hcl(h, c, l)
+	for !shade.IsValid() && c > 0 {
+		c -= autoShadeChromaStep
+		shade = colorful.Hcl(h, c, l)
+	}
+
+	return shade.Clamped()
 }
 
 // gradientCellCache memoizes interpolation results keyed on the RESOLVED stop colors
diff --git a/src/prompt/engine.go b/src/prompt/engine.go
index 02ad067f..1751b121 100644
--- a/src/prompt/engine.go
+++ b/src/prompt/engine.go
@@ -447,7 +447,53 @@ func resolvePaletteReference(c color.Ansi) color.Ansi {
 		return c
 	}
 
-	return resolved
+	if !resolved.IsGradient() {
+		return resolved
+	}
+
+	return resolveGradientStopReferences(resolved)
+}
+
+// resolveGradientStopReferences expands a palette reference (p:name) used as an
+// individual STOP inside a gradient, e.g. linear-gradient(p:teal): the whole-string
+// resolve above only catches a bare "p:name" that itself resolves to a gradient, so a
+// palette-referenced stop reaches GradientLast, separators, diamond caps, and the
+// parentBackground/parentForeground chain in keywords.go as a raw, unresolvable
+// "p:name" string — none of those have resolver access to expand it themselves. A
+// keyword stop (parentBackground, foreground, accent, ...) or literal hex stop is not
+// a palette key and passes through Resolve unchanged, so this only ever rewrites actual
+// palette references. A stop that resolves to a gradient itself (a palette entry holding
+// a gradient) is left as its raw "p:name" text: GradientStops already rejects a nested
+// gradient, so it must degrade through the normal per-stop resolve-and-skip path in
+// GradientCells instead, same as before this function existed.
+func resolveGradientStopReferences(c color.Ansi) color.Ansi {
+	stops := c.GradientStops()
+	if len(stops) == 0 {
+		return c
+	}
+
+	resolvedStops := make([]string, len(stops))
+	changed := false
+
+	for i, stop := range stops {
+		resolved, err := terminal.Colors.Resolve(stop)
+		if err != nil || resolved.IsGradient() {
+			resolvedStops[i] = stop.String()
+			continue
+		}
+
+		if resolved != stop {
+			changed = true
+		}
+
+		resolvedStops[i] = resolved.String()
+	}
+
+	if !changed {
+		return c
+	}
+
+	return color.Ansi("linear-gradient(" + strings.Join(resolvedStops, ", ") + ")")
 }

git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(color): auto-shade single-stop gradients

A single-stop linear-gradient(#color) previously collapsed to a
useless solid fallback. Turn it into a real two-stop gradient
running from the exact configured color to a shade of it, so a
segment too narrow for a real two-stop gradient still gets a
gradient effect from one configured color.

The shade drops HCL lightness only, by a fraction of the base
color's own lightness (clamped so dark bases still shift and
light ones don't overshoot), walking chroma down only as far as
the sRGB gamut forces. Blending toward black instead pulled
chroma down with it, reading as the color going muddy rather than
deepening.

GradientLast mirrors the same shading on the raw stop text, so
powerline separators, diamond caps, and parentBackground/
parentForeground line up with the color the body actually ends
on. That in turn exposed a gap for palette-referenced stops
(linear-gradient(p:teal)): the whole-string palette resolve in
resolvePaletteReference only expands a bare "p:name" that itself
resolves to a gradient, not a "p:name" used as one stop inside a
gradient literal, so GradientLast had no hex to shade and fell
back to the raw, unshaded base color. Resolve each stop's palette
reference upfront in the engine so every consumer sees a
shadeable value.
EOF

content.text:
Committed as `8cd88103`. Squashed clean, single feat commit.

Two real fixes landed:
1. **Bug** — `linear-gradient(p:teal)` (your exact config) had a palette reference that `GradientLast()` couldn't shade, so the trailing diamond cap fell back to the raw bright base while the body darkened. Fixed by resolving palette refs inside gradient stops upfront in `engine.go`.
2. **Formula** — swapped blend-toward-black (muddies chroma) for hue/chroma-preserving lightness drop, so the shade reads as deliberate depth rather than desaturation.

Verified against your live `~/.posh.omp.jsonc`, both success and error states: body runs `#179299 → #0e848b → #04777d`, trailing cap lands exactly on `#04777d` — no more snap-back.
The shading is now correct, but I still feel the individual cell steps are too large (I can see anctual visual difference per shell rather than a smooth gradient).

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(color): auto-shade single-stop gradients

A single-stop linear-gradient(#color) previously collapsed to a
useless solid fallback. Turn it into a real two-stop gradient
running from the exact configured color to a shade of it, so a
segment too narrow for a real two-stop gradient still gets a
gradient effect from one configured color.

The shade drops HCL lightness only, by a fraction of the base
color's own lightness (clamped so dark bases still shift and
light ones don't overshoot), walking chroma down only as far as
the sRGB gamut forces. Blending toward black instead pulled
chroma down with it, reading as the color going muddy rather than
deepening. The clamp range is tuned so a 2-3 cell segment - the
common case a single-stop gradient exists for - lands its
per-step jump close to a just-noticeable-difference instead of a
hard color swap; GradientLast has no cell count to work with, so
the total base-to-shade delta can't scale with segment width.

GradientLast mirrors the same shading on the raw stop text, so
powerline separators, diamond caps, and parentBackground/
parentForeground line up with the color the body actually ends
on. That in turn exposed a gap for palette-referenced stops
(linear-gradient(p:teal)): the whole-string palette resolve in
resolvePaletteReference only expands a bare "p:name" that itself
resolves to a gradient, not a "p:name" used as one stop inside a
gradient literal, so GradientLast had no hex to shade and fell
back to the raw, unshaded base color. Resolve each stop's palette
reference upfront in the engine so every consumer sees a
shadeable value.
EOF
)"
git log --oneline -3

content.text:
Committed as `14170c9e`, squashed clean.

Root cause of the banding: total base-to-shade delta was fixed (`GradientLast` has no cell count to work with, so it can't scale per-cell), and at your 3-cell segment that split into ~2.3x-JND jumps per step — clearly perceptible swaps, not a blend. Cut the delta clamp range roughly in half (0.18/0.06/0.12 → 0.08/0.025/0.05), tuned so a 2-3 cell segment's per-step jump lands near the just-noticeable-difference threshold instead of well above it.

Verified against your live config: `23,146,153 → 15,140,147 → 5,134,141` (was `→14,132,139→4,119,125` before), edges still exact match on both success and error states.
can we change the implementation to use light-gradient to move towards a light grade witha s ingle color and dark-gradient to move to a dark shade (as we have it now)?

result.output:
1	package color
2	
3	import (
4		"math"
5		"strconv"
6		"strings"
7	
8		"github.com/gookit/color"
9		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/log"
10		"github.com/lucasb-eyer/go-colorful"
11	)
12	
13	const (
14		gradientPrefix = "linear-gradient("
15		gradientSuffix = ")"
16	)
17	
18	// IsGradient reports whether c is a gradient definition, e.g. `linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF)`.
19	func (c Ansi) IsGradient() bool {
20		return strings.HasPrefix(c.String(), gradientPrefix)
21	}
22	
23	// GradientStops performs syntax parsing only: it splits the comma-separated stop list inside
24	// `linear-gradient(...)` and trims whitespace around each stop. It does not resolve palette
25	// references or validate that a stop is a color. Returns nil when c is not a gradient, the
26	// closing paren is missing, the body contains a nested paren (angle/direction syntax is
27	// reserved but not implemented), or any stop is empty.
28	func (c Ansi) GradientStops() []Ansi {
29		if !c.IsGradient() {
30			return nil
31		}
32	
33		value := c.String()
34		if !strings.HasSuffix(value, gradientSuffix) {
35			return nil
36		}
37	
38		body := value[len(gradientPrefix) : len(value)-len(gradientSuffix)]
39		if strings.ContainsAny(body, "()") {
40			return nil
41		}
42	
43		parts := strings.Split(body, ",")
44		stops := make([]Ansi, 0, len(parts))
45	
46		for _, part := range parts {
47			stop := strings.TrimSpace(part)
48			if stop == "" {
49				return nil
50			}
51	
52			stops = append(stops, Ansi(stop))
53		}
54	
55		return stops
56	}
57	
58	// GradientFirst returns the first stop of the gradient. It returns c unchanged when c is not
59	// a gradient, or when the gradient syntax is invalid.
60	func (c Ansi) GradientFirst() Ansi {
61		stops := c.GradientStops()
62		if len(stops) == 0 {
63			return c
64		}
65	
66		return stops[0]
67	}
68	
69	// GradientLast returns the last stop of the gradient. It returns c unchanged when c is not
70	// a gradient, or when the gradient syntax is invalid. A single-stop (auto-shade) gradient's
71	// last stop is the same darkened shade GradientCells renders the segment's last cell as,
72	// so separators, diamond caps, and parentBackground/parentForeground line up with the body
73	// instead of jumping back to the unshaded configured color.
74	func (c Ansi) GradientLast() Ansi {
75		stops := c.GradientStops()
76		if len(stops) == 0 {
77			return c
78		}
79	
80		last := stops[len(stops)-1]
81	
82		if len(stops) == 1 {
83			if clr, err := colorful.Hex(last.String()); err == nil {
84				return Ansi(autoShade(clr).Hex())
85			}
86		}
87	
88		return last
89	}
90	
91	// GradientCells resolves each stop of the gradient c — keywords like parentBackground against
92	// current/parents, palette references through resolver — parses the result as a hex color, and
93	// interpolates across cells steps in HCL space. It returns one ready-to-print ANSI color code
94	// per cell, honoring the package-level TrueColor flag: a truecolor escape when true, a gookit
95	// C256 downgrade when false. cells == 1 returns only the first stop. Returns nil when fewer
96	// than two stops resolve to a valid color; the caller falls back to a single collapsed color
97	// per the gradient-invalid rule.
98	func GradientCells(c Ansi, cells int, resolver String, isBackground bool, current *Set, parents []*Set) []Ansi {
99		if cells <= 0 {
100			return nil
101		}
102	
103		stops := c.GradientStops()
104		if len(stops) == 0 {
105			log.Errorf("gradient %s: invalid syntax, expected linear-gradient(stop, stop, ...)", c)
106			return nil
107		}
108	
109		colors := make([]colorful.Color, 0, len(stops))
110	
111		for _, stop := range stops {
112			// a keyword stop (parentBackground, foreground, ...) resolves against the
113			// segment context first; a parent gradient collapses to its last stop there.
114			resolved := stop.Resolve(current, parents)
115	
116			resolved, err := resolver.Resolve(resolved)
117			if err != nil {
118				log.Errorf("gradient %s: unable to resolve stop %s: %s", c, stop, err)
119				continue
120			}
121	
122			// the OS accent color only resolves in ToAnsi, to a truecolor payload
123			// rather than hex; parseTrueColor recovers the RGB triplet from it.
124			if resolved == Accent {
125				resolved = resolver.ToAnsi(Accent, false)
126			}
127	
128			clr, err := colorful.Hex(resolved.String())
129			if err != nil {
130				var ok bool
131				if clr, ok = parseTrueColor(resolved); !ok {
132					log.Errorf("gradient %s: stop %s does not resolve to a color, only hex colors, palette references, and keywords resolving to a color can be interpolated", c, stop)
133					continue
134				}
135			}
136	
137			colors = append(colors, clr)
138		}
139	
140		// a single explicit stop, e.g. linear-gradient(#3465a4), is an auto-shade request:
141		// turn the one resolved color into a real two-stop gradient running from that exact
142		// color to a fixed darker shade of it, reusing the interpolation below unchanged. The
143		// segment's own cell count (fewer cells = coarser steps) already gives narrow segments
144		// a gentler ramp, same as any other gradient; see GradientLast for the matching edge.
145		if len(stops) == 1 {
146			if len(colors) == 0 {
147				log.Errorf("gradient %s: stop does not resolve to a color", c)
148				return nil
149			}
150	
151			colors = []colorful.Color{colors[0], autoShade(colors[0])}
152		}
153	
154		if len(colors) < 2 {
155			log.Errorf("gradient %s: needs at least two valid stops, rendering the last stop as a solid color", c)
156			return nil
157		}
158	
159		if cached, ok := gradientCellCache[gradientKey(colors, cells, isBackground)]; ok {
160			return cached
161		}
162	
163		if cells == 1 {
164			return cacheGradientCells(colors, cells, isBackground, []Ansi{ansiFromColorful(colors[0], isBackground)})
165		}
166	
167		segments := len(colors) - 1
168		result := make([]Ansi, cells)
169	
170		for i := range cells {
171			position := float64(i) / float64(cells-1) * float64(segments)
172	
173			segment := int(position)
174			if segment >= segments {
175				segment = segments - 1
176			}
177	
178			blended := colors[segment].BlendHcl(colors[segment+1], position-float64(segment)).Clamped()
179			result[i] = ansiFromColorful(blended, isBackground)
180		}
181	
182		return cacheGradientCells(colors, cells, isBackground, result)
183	}
184	
185	// autoShadeDeltaSlope/Floor/Ceiling shape the lightness drop autoShade applies, as a
186	// fraction of the base color's own HCL lightness: proportional (so a near-black base
187	// still shifts visibly and a near-white one doesn't overshoot), clamped to a floor (a
188	// visible ramp even on already-dark colors) and a ceiling (no harsh drop on light ones).
189	// GradientLast has no cell count to work with (see its doc comment), so the total
190	// base-to-shade delta is fixed regardless of segment width; these values are tuned so a
191	// 2-3 cell segment - the common case a single-stop gradient exists for - lands its
192	// per-step jump close to a JND (~2.3 CIE76 ΔE) rather than reading as a hard color swap.
193	const (
194		autoShadeDeltaSlope   = 0.08
195		autoShadeDeltaFloor   = 0.025
196		autoShadeDeltaCeiling = 0.05
197		autoShadeMinLightness = 0.02
198		autoShadeChromaStep   = 0.005
199	)
200	
201	// autoShade derives a single-stop (auto-shade) gradient's second stop: same hue, same
202	// chroma other than what the sRGB gamut forces away, lightness dropped by a clamped
203	// fraction of the base's own lightness. Blending toward black (the previous approach)
204	// pulls chroma down linearly alongside lightness, which reads as the color going muddy
205	// rather than deepening; walking chroma down only as far as IsValid demands keeps the
206	// hue exact and preserves as much saturation as the gamut allows at the new lightness.
207	// GradientLast mirrors this on the raw (unresolved) stop text so a segment's trailing
208	// separator/cap matches the color GradientCells renders the last cell as.
209	func autoShade(base colorful.Color) colorful.Color {
210		h, c, l := base.Hcl()
211	
212		delta := math.Max(autoShadeDeltaFloor, math.Min(autoShadeDeltaCeiling, autoShadeDeltaSlope*l))
213		l = math.Max(autoShadeMinLightness, l-delta)
214	
215		shade := colorful.Hcl(h, c, l)
216		for !shade.IsValid() && c > 0 {
217			c -= autoShadeChromaStep
218			shade = colorful.Hcl(h, c, l)
219		}
220	
221		return shade.Clamped()
222	}
223	
224	// gradientCellCache memoizes interpolation results keyed on the RESOLVED stop colors
225	// (keyword and palette stops resolve before the key is built, so context changes miss
226	// the cache correctly), the cell count, the channel, and the TrueColor mode. Prompt
227	// rendering is single-threaded (see the terminal writer's package state), so a plain
228	// map suffices. Bounded to keep long-lived daemons from growing it unchecked.
229	var gradientCellCache = make(map[string][]Ansi)
230	
231	const gradientCellCacheLimit = 128
232	
233	func gradientKey(colors []colorful.Color, cells int, isBackground bool) string {
234		buf := make([]byte, 0, 8+len(colors)*12)
235	
236		for _, clr := range colors {
237			r, g, b := clr.RGB255()
238			buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(r), 10)
239			buf = append(buf, ';')
240			buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(g), 10)
241			buf = append(buf, ';')
242			buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(b), 10)
243			buf = append(buf, ',')
244		}
245	
246		buf = strconv.AppendInt(buf, int64(cells), 10)
247	
248		if isBackground {
249			buf = append(buf, 'b')
250		}
251	
252		if TrueColor {
253			buf = append(buf, 't')
254		}
255	
256		return string(buf)
257	}
258	
259	func cacheGradientCells(colors []colorful.Color, cells int, isBackground bool, result []Ansi) []Ansi {
260		if len(gradientCellCache) >= gradientCellCacheLimit {
261			gradientCellCache = make(map[string][]Ansi)
262		}
263	
264		gradientCellCache[gradientKey(colors, cells, isBackground)] = result
265		return result
266	}
267	
268	// parseTrueColor parses a truecolor ANSI payload ("38;2;r;g;b" or "48;2;r;g;b") back into
269	// a colorful.Color. The OS accent color resolves to this form instead of hex.
270	func parseTrueColor(c Ansi) (colorful.Color, bool) {
271		parts := strings.Split(c.String(), ";")
272		if len(parts) != 5 || (parts[0] != "38" && parts[0] != "48") || parts[1] != "2" {
273			return colorful.Color{}, false
274		}
275	
276		rgb := make([]uint8, 3)
277		for i, part := range parts[2:] {
278			val, err := strconv.ParseUint(part, 10, 8)
279			if err != nil {
280				return colorful.Color{}, false
281			}
282	
283			rgb[i] = uint8(val)
284		}
285	
286		return colorful.Color{R: float64(rgb[0]) / 255.0, G: float64(rgb[1]) / 255.0, B: float64(rgb[2]) / 255.0}, true
287	}
288	
289	// ansiFromColorful converts an interpolated HCL color to a ready-to-print ANSI code, honoring
290	// the package-level TrueColor flag. The truecolor payload is built with strconv appends
291	// rather than gookit's fmt.Sprintf path: one allocation per cell instead of four, on what
292	// is the gradient hot path's dominant allocation site.
293	func ansiFromColorful(c colorful.Color, isBackground bool) Ansi {
294		r, g, b := c.RGB255()
295	
296		if !TrueColor {
297			return Ansi(color.RGB(r, g, b, isBackground).C256().String())
298		}
299	
300		buf := make([]byte, 0, 16)
301	
302		if isBackground {
303			buf = append(buf, "48;2;"...)
304		} else {
305			buf = append(buf, "38;2;"...)
306		}
307	
308		buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(r), 10)
309		buf = append(buf, ';')
310		buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(g), 10)
311		buf = append(buf, ';')
312		buf = strconv.AppendUint(buf, uint64(b), 10)
313	
314		return Ansi(buf)
315	}

147			{Case: "one invalid stop of two", Color: "linear-gradient(#FF0000, notacolor)", Cells: 3},
148			{Case: "single unresolvable stop", Color: "linear-gradient(notacolor)", Cells: 3},
149			{Case: "not a gradient", Color: "#FF0000", Cells: 3},
150			{Case: "invalid syntax", Color: "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF", Cells: 3},
151			{Case: "zero cells", Color: "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF)", Cells: 0},
152		}
153	
154		for _, tc := range cases {
155			result := GradientCells(tc.Color, tc.Cells, &Defaults{}, false, nil, nil)
156			assert.Nil(t, result, tc.Case)
157		}
158	}
159	
160	// TestGradientCellsSingleStopAutoShade verifies linear-gradient(#color) — a single
161	// explicit stop — spreads into a two-stop gradient running from the exact configured
162	// color to a fixed darker shade of it, instead of collapsing like an ordinary invalid
163	// (< 2 stop) gradient. The first cell must be the unmodified configured color (matching
164	// GradientFirst) and the last must match GradientLast, so separators/caps line up.
165	func TestGradientCellsSingleStopAutoShade(t *testing.T) {
166		gradient := Ansi("linear-gradient(#3465A4)")
167		result := GradientCells(gradient, 5, &Defaults{}, false, nil, nil)
168		assert.Len(t, result, 5)
169	
170		assert.Equal(t, Ansi("38;2;52;101;164"), result[0], "the first cell must be the unmodified configured color")
171	
172		expectedLast := GradientCells(Ansi("linear-gradient(#3465A4, "+gradient.GradientLast().String()+")"), 5, &Defaults{}, false, nil, nil)
173		assert.Equal(t, expectedLast[len(expectedLast)-1], result[len(result)-1], "the last cell must match GradientLast, the color separators/caps use")
174	
175		assert.NotEqual(t, result[0], result[len(result)-1], "the segment must actually shade, not render solid")
176	}
177	
178	// TestGradientCellsSingleStopAutoShadeSingleCell verifies a single-cell segment (too
179	// narrow to show any blend) renders the configured color unmodified, not a shaded
180	// endpoint.
181	func TestGradientCellsSingleStopAutoShadeSingleCell(t *testing.T) {
182		result := GradientCells("linear-gradient(#3465A4)", 1, &Defaults{}, false, nil, nil)
183		assert.Equal(t, []Ansi{"38;2;52;101;164"}, result)
184	}
185	
186	// TestGradientLastSingleStopAutoShade verifies GradientLast mirrors GradientCells' shading
187	// for a hex single stop, and falls back to the raw stop for one it can't shade without a
188	// resolver (keyword, palette reference).
189	func TestGradientLastSingleStopAutoShade(t *testing.T) {
190		unshadeable := "can't be shaded without a resolver, so it passes through unchanged"
191	
192		assert.Equal(t, Ansi("#295d9b"), Ansi("linear-gradient(#3465A4)").GradientLast(), "hex stop shades to the same color GradientCells renders the last cell as")
193		assert.Equal(t, Ansi("parentBackground"), Ansi("linear-gradient(parentBackground)").GradientLast(), "a keyword "+unshadeable)
194		assert.Equal(t, Ansi("p:red"), Ansi("linear-gradient(p:red)").GradientLast(), "a palette reference "+unshadeable)
195	}
196	
197	func TestGradientCellsColor256Fallback(t *testing.T) {
198		origTrueColor := TrueColor
199		t.Cleanup(func() { TrueColor = origTrueColor })
200		TrueColor = false
201	
202		result := GradientCells("linear-gradient(#AABBCC, #AABBCC)", 3, &Defaults{}, false, nil, nil)
203	
204		assert.Len(t, result, 3)
205		for i, cell := range result {
206			assert.Equal(t, Ansi("38;5;146"), cell, fmt.Sprintf("cell %d", i))
207		}
208	}
209	
210	// TestGradientCellsKeywordStops verifies keyword stops resolve against the segment
211	// context before interpolation: parentBackground picks up the parent's color (a parent
212	// gradient collapses to its last stop), and keywords without a hex resolution
213	// invalidate the stop.
214	func TestGradientCellsKeywordStops(t *testing.T) {
215		parents := []*Set{{Background: "#112233", Foreground: "#445566"}}
216		gradientParents := []*Set{{Background: "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF)", Foreground: "#445566"}}
217		current := &Set{Background: "#778899", Foreground: "#AABBCC"}
218	
219		cases := []struct {
220			Current  *Set
221			Case     string
222			Color    Ansi
223			Expected Ansi
224			Parents  []*Set
225		}{
226			{
227				Case:     "parentBackground stop resolves to parent color",
228				Color:    "linear-gradient(parentBackground, #FFFFFF)",
229				Parents:  parents,
230				Expected: "38;2;17;34;51",
231			},
232			{
233				Case:     "parentBackground stop collapses a parent gradient to its last stop",
234				Color:    "linear-gradient(parentBackground, #FFFFFF)",
235				Parents:  gradientParents,
236				Expected: "38;2;0;0;255",
237			},
238			{
239				Case:     "foreground stop resolves to the current foreground",
240				Color:    "linear-gradient(foreground, #000000)",
241				Current:  current,
242				Expected: "38;2;170;187;204",
243			},
244		}
245	
246		for _, tc := range cases {
247			result := GradientCells(tc.Color, 2, &Defaults{}, false, tc.Current, tc.Parents)
248			assert.Len(t, result, 2, tc.Case)
249			if len(result) == 2 {
250				assert.Equal(t, tc.Expected, result[0], tc.Case)
251			}
252		}
253	
254		// a keyword with no context resolves to transparent, not a hex color
255		assert.Nil(t, GradientCells("linear-gradient(parentBackground, #FFFFFF)", 2, &Defaults{}, false, nil, nil), "keyword without context invalidates the stop")
256		assert.Nil(t, GradientCells("linear-gradient(transparent, #FFFFFF)", 2, &Defaults{}, false, current, parents), "transparent is never a valid stop")
257	}
258	
259	// TestGradientCellsAccentStop verifies the accent keyword works as a stop: it resolves
260	// through ToAnsi to a truecolor payload, which parseTrueColor recovers. An unresolved
261	// accent (empty Set) invalidates the stop instead of erroring hard.
262	func TestGradientCellsAccentStop(t *testing.T) {
263		resolver := &Defaults{
264			accent: &Set{
265				Foreground: "38;2;0;120;215",
266				Background: "48;2;0;120;215",
267			},
268		}
269	
270		result := GradientCells("linear-gradient(accent, #FFFFFF)", 2, resolver, false, nil, nil)
271		assert.Len(t, result, 2)
272		if len(result) == 2 {
273			assert.Equal(t, Ansi("38;2;0;120;215"), result[0], "first cell must be the accent color")
274			assert.Equal(t, Ansi("38;2;255;255;255"), result[1])
275		}
276	
277		assert.Nil(t, GradientCells("linear-gradient(accent, #FFFFFF)", 2, &Defaults{}, false, nil, nil), "unresolved accent invalidates the stop")
278	}
279	
280	func TestParseTrueColor(t *testing.T) {
281		cases := []struct {
282			Case  string
283			Input Ansi
284			OK    bool
285		}{
286			{Case: "foreground payload", Input: "38;2;255;0;128", OK: true},
287			{Case: "background payload", Input: "48;2;0;120;215", OK: true},
288			{Case: "256-color payload", Input: "38;5;146", OK: false},
289			{Case: "hex string", Input: "#FFFFFF", OK: false},
290			{Case: "out of range", Input: "38;2;300;0;0", OK: false},
291			{Case: "empty", Input: "", OK: false},
292		}
293	
294		for _, tc := range cases {
295			_, ok := parseTrueColor(tc.Input)
296			assert.Equal(t, tc.OK, ok, tc.Case)
297		}
298	}

2	
3	import (
4		"strings"
5		"testing"
6	
7		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/color"
8		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/config"

12		"github.com/stretchr/testify/assert"
13	)
14	
15	// gradientStops is the two-stop gradient used across these tests; red -> blue
16	// keeps the first/last stops trivially distinguishable in assertions.
17	const gradientStops = color.Ansi("linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF)")
18	
19	// TestGetPowerlineColorGradient covers engine.go's getPowerlineColor: the
20	// powerline separator symbol sits at the previous segment's right edge, so a
21	// gradient background must collapse to its last stop, never the first.
22	func TestGetPowerlineColorGradient(t *testing.T) {
23		previous := &config.Segment{Type: "text", Style: config.Powerline, Background: gradientStops}
24		active := &config.Segment{Type: "text", Style: config.Powerline, Background: "green"}
25	
26		engine := &Engine{previousActiveSegment: previous, activeSegment: active}
27	
28		got := engine.getPowerlineColor()
29	
30		assert.Equal(t, gradientStops.GradientLast(), got, "powerline separator color must be the gradient's last stop")
31		assert.NotEqual(t, gradientStops.GradientFirst(), got, "powerline separator color must not be the gradient's first stop")
32	}
33	
34	// TestGetPowerlineColorSingleStopAutoShade covers the bug report behind the auto-shade
35	// fix: a single-stop gradient's powerline separator must render the same shaded color
36	// GradientCells renders the segment's last cell as, not the unshaded configured color -
37	// otherwise the separator visibly jumps back to a different shade right after the body.
38	func TestGetPowerlineColorSingleStopAutoShade(t *testing.T) {
39		singleStop := color.Ansi("linear-gradient(#3465A4)")
40		previous := &config.Segment{Type: "text", Style: config.Powerline, Background: singleStop}
41		active := &config.Segment{Type: "text", Style: config.Powerline, Background: "green"}
42	
43		engine := &Engine{previousActiveSegment: previous, activeSegment: active}
44	
45		got := engine.getPowerlineColor()
46	
47		assert.Equal(t, singleStop.GradientLast(), got, "powerline separator must be the auto-shaded last stop")
48		assert.NotEqual(t, singleStop.GradientFirst(), got, "powerline separator must not be the unshaded configured color")
49	}
50	
51	// TestGetPowerlineColorPaletteReferencedStop pins the fix for a palette reference used
52	// as an individual gradient STOP rather than the whole gradient value:
53	// linear-gradient(p:teal) must resolve p:teal before GradientLast shades it, so the
54	// powerline separator matches the auto-shaded color GradientCells renders the segment's
55	// last cell as, instead of the raw "p:teal" text GradientLast previously couldn't shade
56	// without a resolver (it fell through unshaded to the bright, unmodified base color).
57	func TestGetPowerlineColorPaletteReferencedStop(t *testing.T) {
58		engine := New(&runtime.Flags{IsPrimary: true})
59		terminal.String()
60	
61		palette := color.Palette{"teal": "#179299"}
62		origColors := terminal.Colors
63		terminal.Colors = color.MakeColors(palette, false, "", engine.Env)
64		t.Cleanup(func() { terminal.Colors = origColors })
65	
66		previous := &config.Segment{Type: "text", Style: config.Powerline, Background: "linear-gradient(p:teal)"}
67		active := &config.Segment{Type: "text", Style: config.Powerline, Background: "green"}
68	
69		engine.previousActiveSegment = previous
70		engine.activeSegment = active
71	
72		got := engine.getPowerlineColor()
73	
74		assert.Equal(t, color.Ansi("#179299").GradientFirst(), color.Ansi("#179299"), "sanity: base color unchanged by GradientFirst")
75		assert.NotEqual(t, color.Ansi("#179299"), got, "the separator must not render the raw, unshaded base color")
76	
77		unresolvedLast := color.Ansi("linear-gradient(p:teal)").GradientLast()
78		assert.NotEqual(t, unresolvedLast, got, "GradientLast on the raw p:teal stop can't shade without a resolver; the engine must resolve the palette reference first")
79	
80		resolvedLast := color.Ansi("linear-gradient(#179299)").GradientLast()
81		assert.Equal(t, resolvedLast, got, "the separator must match the auto-shaded color GradientCells renders the last body cell as")
82	}
83	
84	// TestWriteSeparatorTrailingDiamondGradient covers writeSeparator's final
85	// trailing-diamond branch: the glyph sits at the segment's right edge, so a
86	// gradient background must render as its last stop rather than the writer's
87	// default cells==1 (first stop) behavior for a single-cell glyph.
88	func TestWriteSeparatorTrailingDiamondGradient(t *testing.T) {
89		render := func(background color.Ansi) string {
90			engine := New(&runtime.Flags{IsPrimary: true})
91			terminal.String() // drain any state left over from a previous case
92			terminal.ParentColors = nil
93	
94			segment := &config.Segment{
95				Type:            "text",
96				Template:        "X",
97				Style:           config.Diamond,
98				TrailingDiamond: "",
99				Foreground:      "white",
100				Background:      background,
101			}
102	
103			assert.NoError(t, segment.MapSegmentWithWriter(engine.Env))
104			segment.Render(0, true)
105	
106			engine.setActiveSegment(segment)
107			engine.writeSeparator(true)
108	
109			out, _ := terminal.String()
110			return out
111		}
112	
113		gradientOut := render(gradientStops)
114		lastStopOut := render(gradientStops.GradientLast())
115		firstStopOut := render(gradientStops.GradientFirst())
116	
117		assert.Equal(t, lastStopOut, gradientOut, "trailing diamond must render the gradient's last stop")
118		assert.NotEqual(t, firstStopOut, gradientOut, "trailing diamond must not render the gradient's first stop")
119	}
120	
121	// TestRenderActiveSegmentDiamondPreviousGradient covers renderActiveSegment's
122	// diamond branch: when the previous segment has no trailing diamond, the next
123	// segment's leading diamond is drawn against the previous segment's
124	// background - its right edge - so a gradient there must collapse to its
125	// last stop.
126	func TestRenderActiveSegmentDiamondPreviousGradient(t *testing.T) {
127		const leadingGlyph = ""
128	
129		leadingDiamondOutput := func(previousBackground color.Ansi) string {
130			engine := New(&runtime.Flags{IsPrimary: true})
131			terminal.String()
132	
133			// TrailingDiamond intentionally left empty so HasEmptyDiamondAtEnd() is true.
134			previous := &config.Segment{
135				Type:       "text",
136				Template:   "P",
137				Style:      config.Diamond,
138				Foreground: "white",
139				Background: previousBackground,
140			}
141			assert.NoError(t, previous.MapSegmentWithWriter(engine.Env))
142			previous.Render(0, true)
143	
144			engine.setActiveSegment(previous)
145			engine.renderActiveSegment()
146			terminal.String() // drain the previous segment's own output
147	
148			next := &config.Segment{
149				Type:           "text",
150				Template:       "N",
151				Style:          config.Diamond,
152				LeadingDiamond: leadingGlyph,
153				Foreground:     "white",
154				Background:     "green",
155			}
156			assert.NoError(t, next.MapSegmentWithWriter(engine.Env))
157			next.Render(1, true)
158	
159			engine.setActiveSegment(next)
160			engine.renderActiveSegment()
161	
162			out, _ := terminal.String()
163	
164			idx := strings.Index(out, leadingGlyph)
165			if !assert.GreaterOrEqual(t, idx, 0, "leading diamond glyph not found in output") {
166				return out
167			}
168	
169			return out[:idx+len(leadingGlyph)]
170		}
171	
172		gradientOut := leadingDiamondOutput(gradientStops)
173		lastStopOut := leadingDiamondOutput(gradientStops.GradientLast())
174		firstStopOut := leadingDiamondOutput(gradientStops.GradientFirst())
175	
176		assert.Equal(t, lastStopOut, gradientOut, "next segment's leading diamond must pick up the previous gradient's last stop")
177		assert.NotEqual(t, firstStopOut, gradientOut, "next segment's leading diamond must not pick up the previous gradient's first stop")
178	}
179	
180	// TestParentBackgroundKeywordCollapsesGradientLastStop is an integration-level
181	// check that a following segment's `parentBackground` keyword resolves a
182	// gradient previous segment to its last stop. The collapse itself lives in
183	// color/keywords.go (T1); this exercises it through the engine's actual
184	// SetParentColors wiring (engine.go's renderActiveSegment) rather than at the
185	// color package unit level.
186	func TestParentBackgroundKeywordCollapsesGradientLastStop(t *testing.T) {
187		render := func(previousBackground color.Ansi) string {
188			engine := New(&runtime.Flags{IsPrimary: true})
189			terminal.String()
190	
191			previous := &config.Segment{
192				Type:       "text",
193				Template:   "P",
194				Style:      config.Powerline,
195				Foreground: "white",
196				Background: previousBackground,
197			}
198			assert.NoError(t, previous.MapSegmentWithWriter(engine.Env))
199			previous.Render(0, true)
200	
201			engine.setActiveSegment(previous)
202			engine.renderActiveSegment()
203			terminal.String()
204	
205			next := &config.Segment{
206				Type:       "text",
207				Template:   "N",
208				Style:      config.Powerline,
209				Foreground: "white",
210				Background: "parentBackground",
211			}
212			assert.NoError(t, next.MapSegmentWithWriter(engine.Env))
213			next.Render(1, true)
214	
215			engine.setActiveSegment(next)
216			engine.renderActiveSegment()
217	
218			out, _ := terminal.String()
219			return out
220		}
221	
222		gradientOut := render(gradientStops)
223		lastStopOut := render(gradientStops.GradientLast())
224	
225		assert.Equal(t, lastStopOut, gradientOut, "parentBackground must collapse a gradient background to its last stop")
226	}

33	## Gradients
34	
35	Anywhere a **Standard color** is expected, you can instead use a `linear-gradient` with two or
36	more comma-separated stops:
37	
38	<Config
39	  data={{
40	    type: "text",
41	    style: "plain",
42	    foreground: "linear-gradient(#FF0000, #0000FF)",
43	  }}
44	/>
45	
46	The stops are spread evenly across the segment's visible text and interpolated per character cell.
47	Each stop can be a hex color or a [Palette reference][palette] (`p:name`), so gradients work with
48	`palette` and `palettes` the same way **Standard colors** do. This applies to `foreground`,
49	`background`, `foreground_templates`, `background_templates`, Palette entries, and `cycle` colors.
50	
51	Powerline separators, diamond caps, and the `parentBackground`/`parentForeground` keywords
52	automatically pick up the matching edge of the gradient, so a segment next to a gradient still
53	connects with the right color.
54	
55	### Auto-shade from a single color
56	
57	A gradient with a single stop, `linear-gradient(#3465A4)`, runs from that exact color to a fixed
58	darker shade of it, letting one configured color produce a gradient effect on a segment too narrow
59	for a real two-stop gradient to render as anything but a solid color, see
60	[Stops and segment width](#stops-and-segment-width).
61	
62	<Config
63	  data={{
64	    type: "text",
65	    style: "plain",
66	    background: "linear-gradient(#3465A4)",
67	  }}
68	/>
69	
70	The first cell always renders the exact configured color; the last renders the darker shade.
71	Everything in between interpolates the same way a two-stop gradient does, so a wide segment shows
72	a gentler ramp and a narrow one a coarser one. A single-cell segment renders the configured color
73	unmodified.
74	
75	### Supported stops
76	
77	A stop must resolve to a hex color before it can be interpolated:
78	
79	| Stop                                                               | Supported |
80	| ------------------------------------------------------------------ | --------- |
81	| Hex color (`#CB4B16`, `#F0A`)                                      | yes       |
82	| [Palette reference][palette] to a hex color (`p:blue`)             | yes       |
83	| `parentBackground`, `parentForeground`, `background`, `foreground` | yes       |
84	| `accent` (Windows and macOS only)                                  | yes       |
85	| ANSI color name (`red`, `lightBlue`)                               | no        |
86	| 256 color palette number (`146`)                                   | no        |
87	| `transparent`                                                      | no        |
88	
89	Keyword stops resolve against the surrounding segments at render time and must land on a hex
90	color; when the previous segment uses a gradient itself, `parentBackground` picks up its last
91	stop, so `linear-gradient(parentBackground, p:yellow)` connects seamlessly to it. On platforms
92	where the OS accent color is unavailable, an `accent` stop is invalid and the gradient falls
93	back like any other invalid stop.
94	
95	A `background` or `foreground` keyword in a [color override](#color-overrides) inside a gradient
96	segment resolves to the gradient's color at that position in the text: a leading
97	`<background,transparent>` cap picks up the first stop, a trailing one the color where the
98	gradient ends, so template caps stay seamless without hardcoding edge colors.
99	
100	A gradient with two or more configured stops that resolves to fewer than two supported colors
101	renders as a solid color: the last stop goes through the regular color pipeline, so
102	`linear-gradient(red, blue)` renders solid blue rather than failing — matching the color its
103	separators and width-collapsed form already use. Rejected stops are reported in `oh-my-posh debug`
104	output. A single _configured_ stop is not this fallback case, it's the
105	[auto-shade](#auto-shade-from-a-single-color) form.
106

### Auto-shade from a single color

`dark-gradient(#3465A4)` and `light-gradient(#3465A4)` take a single color and run from that
exact color to a darker or lighter shade of it, letting one configured color produce a gradient
effect on a segment too narrow for a real two-stop gradient to render as anything but a solid
color, see [Stops and segment width](#stops-and-segment-width).

<Config
  data={{
    type: "text",
    style: "plain",
    background: "dark-gradient(#3465A4)",
  }}
/>

in between interpolates the same way a two-stop gradient does, so a wide segment shows a gentler
ramp and a narrow one a coarser one. A single-cell segment renders the configured color unmodified.
Each takes exactly one color; `dark-gradient`/`light-gradient` with more than one stop is invalid,
same as a `linear-gradient` with fewer than two.

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(color): add dark-gradient/light-gradient for single-color shading

Replace the linear-gradient(#color) single-stop overload with two
explicit forms: dark-gradient(#color) shades toward black,
light-gradient(#color) toward white. linear-gradient reverts to
needing two or more stops, so the auto-shade behavior has its own
name instead of being an implicit side effect of stop count.

Both spread the one configured color into a real two-stop gradient
(same hue, chroma preserved as far as the sRGB gamut allows,
lightness shifted toward the target end by a clamped fraction of
the color's own headroom), giving a segment too narrow for a real
two-stop gradient a gradient effect from just one configured color.

GradientLast/GradientCells share the shading logic through a
shadeDirection so separators, diamond caps, and
parentBackground/parentForeground line up with the body regardless
of which form is used. WithGradientStops rebuilds a gradient string
with the same prefix, so resolving a palette-referenced stop (e.g.
dark-gradient(p:teal)) keeps its dark-gradient semantics instead of
silently becoming a plain linear-gradient.
EOF
)"
git log --oneline -3

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(color): auto-shade single-color gradients with dark-gradient/light-gradient

A single-color gradient effect for segments too narrow for a real
two-stop gradient to render as anything but a solid color:
dark-gradient(#color) runs from that exact color to a darker
shade, light-gradient(#color) to a lighter one.

The shade drops (or raises) HCL lightness only, by a fraction of
the base color's own headroom toward the target end (clamped so
colors already close to it still shift and ones far from it don't
overshoot), walking chroma down only as far as the sRGB gamut
forces. Blending toward black/white directly pulls chroma down
with it, reading as the color going muddy rather than
deepening/brightening. The clamp range is tuned so a 2-3 cell
segment - the common case this exists for - lands its per-step
jump close to a just-noticeable-difference instead of a hard color
swap; GradientLast has no cell count to work with, so the total
base-to-shade delta can't scale with segment width.

GradientLast mirrors the same shading on the raw stop text, so
powerline separators, diamond caps, and parentBackground/
parentForeground line up with the color the body actually ends on.
WithGradientStops rebuilds a gradient string keeping its own prefix
(linear-gradient, dark-gradient, or light-gradient), so resolving a
palette-referenced stop (dark-gradient(p:teal)) keeps its darken
semantics instead of silently becoming a plain linear-gradient -
whole-string palette resolution only ever expanded a bare "p:name"
that itself resolved to a gradient, never a "p:name" used as one
stop inside a gradient literal, so a palette-referenced stop
reached GradientLast unresolved and fell back to the raw,
unshaded base color.

linear-gradient itself goes back to requiring two or more stops;
the single-stop overload it briefly had is now dark-gradient's job.
EOF
)"
git log --oneline -3

262	
263	// autoShadeDeltaSlope/Floor/Ceiling shape the lightness shift autoShade applies, as a
264	// fraction of the base color's own headroom toward black or white (so a color already
265	// close to the target end still shifts visibly, and one far from it doesn't overshoot),
266	// clamped to a floor (a visible ramp even near the target end) and a ceiling (no harsh
267	// jump far from it). GradientLast has no cell count to work with (see its doc comment),
268	// so the total base-to-shade delta is fixed regardless of segment width; these values are
269	// tuned so a 2-3 cell segment - the common case dark-gradient/light-gradient exists for -
270	// lands its per-step jump close to a JND (~2.3 CIE76 ΔE) rather than a hard color swap.
271	const (
272		autoShadeDeltaSlope   = 0.08
273		autoShadeDeltaFloor   = 0.025
274		autoShadeDeltaCeiling = 0.05
275		autoShadeMinLightness = 0.02
276		autoShadeMaxLightness = 0.98
277		autoShadeChromaStep   = 0.005
278	)
279	
280	// autoShade derives a dark-gradient/light-gradient's second stop: same hue, same chroma
281	// other than what the sRGB gamut forces away, lightness shifted toward black (shadeDark)
282	// or white (shadeLight) by a clamped fraction of the base's own headroom in that
283	// direction. Blending toward black/white directly (the previous approach) pulls chroma
284	// along with it, which reads as the color going muddy rather than deepening/brightening;
285	// walking chroma down only as far as IsValid demands keeps the hue exact and preserves as
286	// much saturation as the gamut allows at the new lightness. GradientLast mirrors this on
287	// the raw (unresolved) stop text so a segment's trailing separator/cap matches the color
288	// GradientCells renders the last cell as.
289	func autoShade(base colorful.Color, dir shadeDirection) colorful.Color {
290		h, c, l := base.Hcl()
291	
292		if dir == shadeLight {
293			delta := math.Max(autoShadeDeltaFloor, math.Min(autoShadeDeltaCeiling, autoShadeDeltaSlope*(1-l)))
294			l = math.Min(autoShadeMaxLightness, l+delta)
295		} else {
296			delta := math.Max(autoShadeDeltaFloor, math.Min(autoShadeDeltaCeiling, autoShadeDeltaSlope*l))
297			l = math.Max(autoShadeMinLightness, l-delta)
298		}
299	
300		shade := colorful.Hcl(h, c, l)
301		for !shade.IsValid() && c > 0 {
302			c -= autoShadeChromaStep
303			shade = colorful.Hcl(h, c, l)

content.text: Found it — I compared against the previously-approved narrow tuning, and even a realistic 15-20 cell segment (not just your padded stress test) washes almost to white by ~cells=15, then plateaus there for anything wider. Linear-with-cells was the wrong shape; it needs to saturate. Let's fix with a smooth diminishing-returns curve instead of linear growth + hard clamp.

result.output:
263	// autoShadeStepSlope/Floor/Ceiling shape the PER-CELL-STEP lightness shift autoShade
264	// targets, as a fraction of the base color's own headroom toward black or white (so a
265	// color already close to the target end still shifts visibly, and one far from it
266	// doesn't overshoot), clamped to a floor (a visible ramp even near the target end) and a
267	// ceiling (no harsh per-step jump far from it) tuned to land close to a JND (~2.3 CIE76
268	// ΔE) per step. autoShadeDeltaMax caps the TOTAL base-to-shade delta a wide segment can
269	// reach, so an already-narrow-range color doesn't get crushed toward black/white.
270	const (
271		autoShadeStepSlope    = 0.04
272		autoShadeStepFloor    = 0.0125
273		autoShadeStepCeiling  = 0.025
274		autoShadeDeltaMax     = 0.35
275		autoShadeMinLightness = 0.02
276		autoShadeMaxLightness = 0.98
277		autoShadeChromaStep   = 0.005
278	)
279	
280	// autoShade derives a dark-gradient/light-gradient's second stop: same hue, same chroma
281	// other than what the sRGB gamut forces away, lightness shifted toward black (shadeDark)
282	// or white (shadeLight). The shift is a per-cell-step target (see autoShadeStepSlope)
283	// times steps = max(1, cells-1), so GradientCells(c, cells, ...) always lands its actual
284	// last cell here regardless of width: a 2-3 cell segment gets one gentle step, matching
285	// what narrow segments were tuned to look like, while a wide segment's larger total delta
286	// still reads as a clear gradient instead of fading into an imperceptibly fine ramp -
287	// capped at autoShadeDeltaMax so an extremely wide segment doesn't crush toward black or
288	// white. Blending toward black/white directly (an earlier approach) pulls chroma along
289	// with it, which reads as the color going muddy rather than deepening/brightening;
290	// walking chroma down only as far as IsValid demands keeps the hue exact and preserves as
291	// much saturation as the gamut allows at the new lightness. GradientLastForCells mirrors
292	// this on the raw (unresolved) stop text so a segment's trailing separator/cap matches
293	// the color GradientCells renders the last cell as, for the SAME cells.
294	func autoShade(base colorful.Color, dir shadeDirection, cells int) colorful.Color {
295		h, c, l := base.Hcl()
296	
297		steps := max(1, cells-1)
298	
299		if dir == shadeLight {
300			step := math.Max(autoShadeStepFloor, math.Min(autoShadeStepCeiling, autoShadeStepSlope*(1-l)))
301			delta := math.Min(autoShadeDeltaMax, step*float64(steps))
302			l = math.Min(autoShadeMaxLightness, l+delta)
303		} else {
304			step := math.Max(autoShadeStepFloor, math.Min(autoShadeStepCeiling, autoShadeStepSlope*l))
305			delta := math.Min(autoShadeDeltaMax, step*float64(steps))
306			l = math.Max(autoShadeMinLightness, l-delta)
307		}
308	
309		shade := colorful.Hcl(h, c, l)
310		for !shade.IsValid() && c > 0 {
311			c -= autoShadeChromaStep
312			shade = colorful.Hcl(h, c, l)

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(color): auto-shade single-color gradients with dark-gradient/light-gradient

A single-color gradient effect for segments too narrow for a real
two-stop gradient to render as anything but a solid color:
dark-gradient(#color) runs from that exact color to a darker
shade, light-gradient(#color) to a lighter one.

The shade drops (or raises) HCL lightness only, by a fraction of
the base color's own headroom toward the target end, keeping hue
exact and walking chroma down only as far as the sRGB gamut
forces - blending toward black/white directly pulls chroma down
with it, reading as the color going muddy rather than
deepening/brightening. The reference delta is tuned so a 3-cell
segment - the common case this exists for - lands its shade close
to a just-noticeable-difference rather than a hard color swap; a
width multiplier grows that delta for wider segments as a
saturating curve (not linear growth with a hard clamp - that hit
its clamp by ~15-20 cells and crushed every wider segment to the
exact same near-white/near-black color, which is what "the
gradient stops working on a larger segment" turned out to be),
so a very wide segment still shades further than a moderately
wide one while topping out at a moderate, recognizably-still-the-
same-hue color instead of washing out to white or black.

GradientLastForCells mirrors the same shading (for a given cell
count) on the raw stop text, so powerline separators, diamond
caps, and inline color-override edges (via a new
gradientRenderCells package var in the terminal writer) line up
with the color the body actually ends on for that segment's own
width. GradientLast (no cell count - used by parentBackground/
parentForeground crossing into another segment, and wherever
width isn't known) falls back to the narrowest, gentlest shade.

WithGradientStops rebuilds a gradient string keeping its own
prefix (linear-gradient, dark-gradient, or light-gradient), so
resolving a palette-referenced stop (dark-gradient(p:teal)) keeps
its darken semantics instead of silently becoming a plain
linear-gradient - whole-string palette resolution only ever
expanded a bare "p:name" that itself resolved to a gradient,
never a "p:name" used as one stop inside a gradient literal, so a
palette-referenced stop reached GradientLast unresolved and fell
back to the raw, unshaded base color.

linear-gradient itself goes back to requiring two or more stops;
the single-stop overload it briefly had is now dark-gradient's job.
EOF
)"
git log --oneline -3

content.text:
Committed as `62c5d6c0`.

Root cause: total delta grew linearly with cell count against a hard cap, so anything past ~15-20 cells hit the same clamp and rendered the identical near-white/near-black color regardless of width — that's the "doesn't work anymore." Fixed with a saturating curve: reference tuning is preserved exactly at 3 cells, growth eases toward a moderate cap (max 4x) so wide segments keep shading further as they widen instead of flatlining, and even very wide ones stay recognizably the same hue.

Also plumbed real cell counts into edge consumers that were quietly guessing before (`gradientRenderCells` in the writer for inline overrides, `VisibleCells(segment.Text())` in `backgroundEdge` for separators/diamond caps) — fixed a test panic that surfaced along the way (`backgroundEdge` now calls `segment.Text()`, which needs a rendered segment, not a bare struct).

Verified against your live config at your actual padded width: tail settles around `78,184,191` (still teal) instead of washing to `140,243,250` (near-white).
